AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 2 - Product Overview

2

Product Overview

AVEVA NET Gateways process and convert data from multiple data sources into a

compatible file format that is loaded into AVEVA NET Workhub and Dashboard. The data

sources can be Microsoft Access, Microsoft Excel, Oracle, Documentum and Primavera

Server and many more. The output that the Gateways generate is XML format. These XML

files, also known as Engineering Information and Workflow Model (EIWM) files, enable

the import of source data into AVEVA NET Workhub and Dashboard as a set of objects

using Import Controller. The various datatypes are as follows:

· Structured Data: Proprietary business data formats, including SAP, Primavera and

Microsoft Project

· Unstructured Data: Document formats, such as Word, Excel, PDF and
PowerPoint; Documents held within Documentum or SharePoint

· 2D Data: 2D CAD data formats, including AutoCAD, MicroStation, SmartPlant

P&ID, INTools and AVEVA P&ID

· 3D Data: 3D model formats, such as SmartPlant 3D, PDS 3D, AVEVA PDMS,

AutoPlant 3D, AVEVA Marine and so on

Gateways perform the following major functions:

· Process new information from data sources and add it to the common

information model, which enables AVEVA NET to store a unified and cross-

referenced model of all tags across projects or asset.

· Scan information sources for tagged items, extract them and automatically build

relationships between them.

· Render accurately the source information and apply hotspots to tagged items,

which enables you to easily navigate related information.

Depending on the processing of the data, Gateways can be segregated into the following

types:

· CAD File Processing Gateways: AVEVA NET Gateway for AutoCAD 2D, AVEVA NET

Gateway for AutoCAD 3D and so on

· Document Indexing Gateways: AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway
· DMS Gateways: AVEVA NET Gateway for CMIS
· Data Processing Gateways: AVEVA NET Gateway for Primavera, AVEVA NET Data

Extractor Gateway and so on

Release 5.0.11

Page 8

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 2 - Product Overview

2.1

What is AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway?

The AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway provides a single application through which

you can index files of many types (Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and so on) so that document

to tag associations can be generated for AVEVA NET. The Gateway can if necessary make

use of other applications to index files. The Gateway processes the following types of files:

· Image-only PDF files and with the aid of an external Optical Character

Recognition (OCR) application (for example, Adlib Express Server) and the tags

referenced in the files.

· Searchable PDF files and the tags referenced in the files.
· Textual files (.txt) and the tags referenced in the files.
· Microsoft Office files (.doc/.docx, .xls/.xlsx or .ppt/.pptx) and the tags

referenced in the files.

The Gateway processes document files and extracts the text from the document files and

then searches that text for tags. Usually many document formats (Word, Excel,

PowerPoint, and so on) require a different approach to extract text, see Extracting Text

from Files. You can determine the tags by searching the extracted text for the expected

pattern of a tag using pattern mapping. For more information about pattern mapping, see

Pattern Mapping.

The output of the Gateway is an AVEVA NET format XML file for each source file processed

containing the tag to document associations.

Release 5.0.11

Page 9

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 3 - Getting Started

· To process Excel and Access files through the Gateway, you must install Microsoft
Office on your machine. Microsoft Office and Windows Server must be installed

on the same machine.

· Installation of the Adobe Reader and using an Adobe iFilter to process Adobe PDF
files is no longer required. The functionality for extracting text from Adobe PDF

files is now provided with this gateway using embedded technology.

· Processing of .dwg files requires an iFilter. AVEVA does not supply an iFilter.

However, various vendors of design software that produce DWG exports often

provide them.

· AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway supports the processing of PowerPoint

files.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 3 - Getting Started

3.2.1

Licensing

You can configure the licensing for the Gateway in one of the following ways:

· ALS
· AVEVA's Licensing as a Service (LaaS)

AVEVA Licensing System (ALS)

You must install ALS 4.1 and the appropriate license file before you install the Gateway.

The ALS manages the license for the Gateway:

· You must use the server name of the ALS Console Server on your network, for

example, 1234@Serv1.

· If you do not have an ALS Console Server, you must install ALS 4.1, which is

available on the customer website under AVEVA Licensing.

· Retrieve the necessary ALS licenses for the installation requirements.
· Set the environment variable AVEVA_LICENSE_SERVER_LIST to point to the

name and locations of the ALS license file.

Note: For more information about ALS 4.1, see http://support.aveva.com.

Licensing as a Service (LaaS)

AVEVA's Licensing as a Service (LaaS) is a Cloud-based alternative to the ALS installed on

your premise. LaaS helps you reduce the amount of management required, compared to

managing your own ALS.

For more information about LaaS and how to use it, refer to the AVEVA LaaS Quick Start

Guide.

Before you can use LaaS to license the Gateway, you must be a user in your organisation’s

AVEVA Connect account.

Contact your organisation’s AVEVA Connect Account Administrator to add you as a user.

After you have been added as a user, you can start using LaaS to license the Gateway.

To use LaaS to license the Gateway:

1.

Change your computer's environment variable

AVEVA_LICENSE_SERVER_LIST to https://license.connect.aveva.com.

Click Run to execute the Gateway in Connect interface.

Enter your email and password in the AVEVA Connect Authentication window,

2.

3.

and then click SIGN IN.

A message that you have successfully signed into AVEVA Connect is displayed.

CHAPTER 3 - Getting Started

If your Connect role is authorised to access to the Gateway, a Refresh token

will be stored in the location "C:\ProgramData\AVEVA\.tokenstore" and

the Gateway will be able to process input sources. This Refresh token will then

be used by the Gateway for subsequent runs, including command line and

batch runs, to check out the license using LaaS.

4.

If your Refresh token expires, you will be prompted to create a new one via the

AVEVA Connect authentication pop-up. Refresh tokens expires after a set

interval, normally 6 months for the Gateway.

Notes:

· When using ALS and LaaS together, authentication via ALS takes preference.
· While using command line execution, you must run the Gateway from the user

interface at least once to generate the Refresh token.

3.3

Microsoft Access Database Engine

The Gateway uses the Microsoft Access Database Engine to read Excel/Access files.

You must install the Microsoft Access Database Engine along with Microsoft Office in your

machine to work on the Gateway. You can download the required version of Microsoft

Access Database Engine from:

http://www.microsoft.com/downloads/en/details.aspx?FamilyID=C06B8369-60DD-4B64-

A44B-84B371EDE16D&displaylang=en

Access Database Engine 2010 (64-bit) is compatible with the following system

configurations:

· Microsoft Office 2010 (64-bit), 2013 (64-bit)

If you have installed Microsoft Office 2010/2013 x86 (32-bit), this blocks the installation of

Access Database Engine (64-bit).

You need to perform the following to install Access Database Engine (64-bit):

1. Uninstall Microsoft Office 2010/2013 x86 (32-bit) from your system.

2.

3.

Install Microsoft Office 2010/2013 (64-bit) in your system.

Install Access Database Engine (64-bit).

Note: For the Gateway (5.0.1 version and later), the support for Microsoft Office x86 (32-

bit) operating systems is removed.

3.4

Install the Gateway

To install the Gateway:

1.

From your installation location, double-click the

DocumentIndexingGatewayInstaller.msi file.

2.

3.

Click Next.

Select I accept, and then click Next.

Note: To print a copy of the License Agreement, click Print.

4.

If you want to modify the default installation Location, click Browse.

Note: To find out the available and required disk space for your chosen

features and available drives, click Disk Usage.

5.

Select Everyone or Just me depending on who you want to access the

Gateway.

Click Next.

Click Install. The installation process starts.

Click Finish after the Setup Wizard completes installing the Gateway.

6.

7.

8.

Quiet Installation

To install the Gateway from the Command line:

1.

Type DocumentIndexingGatewayInstaller.msi /? to see all options.

Example of quiet installation with passing folder:

DocumentIndexingGatewayInstaller.msi INSTALLFOLDER="C:

\Program Files\My Folder" /quiet

Change and Repair the Gateway

You can change or repair the Gateway using the Gateway Installer.

To change or repair the Gateway using the Gateway Installer:

From your installation media, double-click the CMISGatewayInstaller.msi

file.

Click Next.

Select one of the following:

2.

3.

a.

Click Change if you want to modify any optional features installed with

the application.

b.

installation, repair any corrupt files, or fix any missing shortcuts or registry

4.

Follow the on-screen instructions.

3.6

Uninstall the Gateway

You can uninstall the Gateway by using one of the following ways:

· From the Control Panel
· Through a silent uninstall
· From the Gateway installer

3.6.1

Uninstall from the Control Panel

To uninstall the Gateway using the control panel:

1. Go to Start, Control Panel, and then click Programs and Features.

2.

3.

Select AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway from the list of programs.

Select Uninstall to remove the Gateway.

3.6.2

Quiet Uninstall

A silent or quiet uninstall is a type of command for a computer program you can use to

uninstall without displaying a progress or without indicating the process.

To install the Gateway from the command line:

1. Navigate to the location of .msi file in the command prompt.

2.

Type /uninstall DocumentIndexingGatewayInstaller.msi or type /x

DocumentIndexingGatewayInstaller.msi to uninstall the gateway.

3.6.3

Uninstall using the Gateway Controller

You can uninstall the Gateway through the DocumentIndexingGatewayInstaller.msi

file by either right-clicking the .msi file or by double-clicking it.

When you right-click the .msi file, click Uninstall and follow the instructions. Project data

is not removed by this uninstallation process.

When you double-click the .msi file, use the following instructions:

1.

2.

3.

4.

5.

Click Next to open the Change Repair and Remove Installation window.

Select the Remove option to uninstall.

If you want to remove the project data, select the Clear application data folder

check box.

To confirm that you want to remove the Gateway, click Remove again.

Follow the on-screen instructions.

Note: All other methods to uninstall the Gateway via the control panel or a silent uninstall

or right-clicking from the .msi file, do not provide this option to clear the project data.

CHAPTER 4 - Creating and Opening Projects

4

Creating and Opening Projects

This chapter provides details on how to create projects and open existing projects.

4.1

Create Projects

A Document Indexing Gateway project is a defined set of configuration settings, stored in

a project configuration file, which can be re-used as required.

An example configuration file (Example Project_64bit.xml) is located in the Example

Project directory in the C:\Program Files\AVEVA\AVEVA NET Gateways\Document

Indexing Gateway folder. Details of the configuration file are explained in Using the

Configuration File.

When you run the application for the first time, the New or Existing Project dialog is

displayed. This is used either to create an entirely new project or import an existing

project created elsewhere. At least one project must be created or imported before you

can use the Gateway.

To access the New or Existing Project dialog after a project has been created, select the

New Configuration option from the Project menu of the AVEVA NET Document Indexing

Gateway dialog.

To create a new project:

1.

2.

3.

Select the Create a New Project option.

In the Name field, enter a unique identifier for the project.

In the Folder field, enter the path and name of the project folder, or click the

Browse button and navigate to the required location.

4.

Click Create Project.

A message is displayed indicating that the project has been created, and the

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway dialog is then displayed with the

new project selected.

Release 5.0.11

Page 17

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 4 - Creating and Opening Projects

4.2

Open an Existing Project
You can open an existing project from either the New or Existing Project dialog or from
the AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway dialog.

4.2.1

From the New or Existing Project Dialog

To import an existing project from the New or Existing Project dialog:

1.

2.

Select the Open an Existing Project option.

Click the Browse icon and browse for the project XML file that contains the

project settings.

3.

Select the Open button.

The Folder field is then populated with name and location of the project

settings file.

4.

Click Open Project.

The AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway dialog is displayed with the

selected project.

4.2.2

From the AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway Dialog

To import an existing project from the AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway dialog:

1.

2.

Select the Select Configuration option from the Project menu.

Browse for the project XML file that contains the project settings and select the

Open option.

The AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway dialog is then displayed with the

selected project opened.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

5

Working with the Gateway Dialog

You can process the Gateway project configuration file using the Gateway dialog.

Using File Menu:

You can create and save a new Project Configuration file, select an existing configuration

file and reload an existing configuration file using the sub-menus under File menu.

· New Configuration: A new project can be created via the New Configuration

option or the button. For more information, see Creating and Opening Projects.
· Select Configuration: Alternatively, you can browse for any project configuration

file and open it from the Select Configuration option under the File menu.
· Save Configuration: The File menu also includes options to save any changes
made to the configuration through the user interface (Save Configuration).

· Reload Configuration: You can reload the last saved configuration for the current

project.

Note: While setting the configuration options through the user interface, you can save

these options so that they do not have to be reset next time when the Gateway is

launched. You can save the current settings by selecting the File, Save Configuration

option. You can at any time revert to the last saved configuration by selecting the File,

Reload Configuration menu option. When you start the Gateway, the last configuration

file used is loaded.

Using Tool Menu:

The Validate Input file Names option enables you to scan the source folder and its sub-

folders, for invalid file and folder names.

Fix Staging Area Folder and File Names:

The AVEVA NET Workhub and Dashboard Import Controller can fail if it comes across

staging area folders or file names in a certain formats for example, if there is a double

space in the folder name. The Gateway can create new staging area file and folder names,

based on the source folder file and folder names, which can cause the Import Controller to

fail.

To help avoid this situation you can use the Tools, Validate Input file Names option that

enables you to scan the source folder, and its sub-folders, for invalid file and folder

names.

Release 5.0.11

Page 19

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

If an invalid name(s) is found, the system reports this and provides you the option to

rectify the problem by renaming the files or folders. If you select the Yes option then the

invalid files or folders are renamed. The configuration file specifies what formats are to be

considered invalid and what should be used to rectify the name. For more information,

see Fix File Names.

Using Help Menu:

You can view the version history, copyright year and third-party components supported by

the Gateway using the Help menu. You can also navigate to the user manual using the

Help menu.

5.1

Use the Project and Locations Folder

When you open the dialog, the configuration file (see Using the Configuration File) of the

last opened (or created) project is loaded automatically. The name of the project and the

name and location of the project configuration file appear in the Name and Location fields

under Project group, respectively.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

You can also select a different project from the list of projects accessible from the Name

field. The following list of projects appears in a tabular form to show the names of projects

created earlier.

The Locations group enables you to define the location of the files to be processed, such

as source folder, destination folder and so on. The following table lists the descriptions of

the fields under Locations group.

Field

Description

Include subfolders Determines whether or not the files in the sub-folders of the source

folder is processed

Source Folder

The folder containing the files to be processed. The application
must have read access to this folder.

Destination Folder

The destination folder represents a location where intermediate
files are created. The application must have write access to this
folder.

Staging Area Folder The AVEVA NET Workhub and Dashboard staging area folder. The

application must have write access to this folder.

Processed Folder

Input files that have been successfully processed are moved to this
folder.

Unprocessed Folder Input files that have been not been successfully processed are

moved to this folder.

Note: If you select the Include Subfolders option, then the Gateway replicates the folder

structure that it finds under the source folder in both the staging area and destination

folders.

5.3

Use the Settings Folder
The following sections explain the procedure to define the project settings and mappings.

The Documents tab under Settings tab shows Mapping and Settings sections, as shown in

the following screenshot:

5.3.1

Mapping

The Mapping tab enables you to configure the pattern mapping file and set the default

context, as mentioned below:

· Pattern Mapping File: The path to the currently selected pattern mapping file. A
browse button and Edit button is provided. See Section Pattern Mapping for

guidance on editing a pattern mapping file.

· Default Context: Defines the AVEVA NET context. Any number of levels of nested
contexts may be specified by separating the context levels by a '|' for example,

SRP|VPD.

Release 5.0.11

Page 22

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

Pattern Mapping

Pattern mapping is used to tag and classify text found within the input files being

processed. An output XML file is created with the format <input file name>_null.xml.

This contains text entities that have been successfully pattern matched. After the

processed files (within Staging Area folder) have been imported into the AVEVA NET

Workhub and Dashboard, these tags are visible within the content viewer and can be

clicked to display the classification of the tagged text.

Pattern mapping is performed by the use of regular expressions as follows:

<Patterns version="5.0">

<Pattern from="[A-Z]{2}\d{1}-\d{1}[\s\S]{1}-[A-Z]{0,2}-\d{0,3}"
to="PipeLine" />
<Pattern from="[A-Z]-\d{1,9}-{0,1}[A-Z]{0,1}" to="PipeLine" />
<Pattern from="\d{0,2}-[A-Z]{0,2}-\d{0,3}-[A-Z]{0,3}" to="Equipment"
separator="*" />
<Pattern from="[A-Z]-d{1,4}" to="Nozzle" separator="*" />
<Pattern from="\d{2}[A-Z]{2}\d{3}[A-Z]-[A-Z]" to="valve" separator="*">
   <Expand Interpolate="true">
      <SubPattern>[A-Z]-[A-Z]</SubPattern>
      <Char>-</Char>
   </Expand>

  </Pattern>
</Patterns>

Regular expression patterns are not specific to this application or to AVEVA and

information on this topic can be found on the Internet.

Insert line breaks: This regex configuration enables you to split the tags based on white

spaces to apply pattern mapping properly.

· If you select the Insert line breaks box then you must apply the regex pattern

without considering the space in the match string.

For example, if you search for ABC 123, it will match with ABC 123 and returns

true.

· If you clear the Insert line breaks box then you must apply the regex pattern

considering the space in the match string.

For example, if you search for ABC 123, it will not match with ABC 123 as it

considers ABC as one string, 123 as another and returns false.

Release 5.0.11

Page 23

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

Pattern Mapping Rules

The following section of the XML is compulsory:

<Patterns>
</Patterns>

Each pattern mapping rule has the following format:

<Pattern from="<regular expression>" to="<Class>" separator="*"
context="xxx"/>

Example: The text string "ABC-123" can be matched using the following patterns:

<Patterns>

<Pattern from="^[A-Z]{3}-\d+" to="Equipment"/>
<Pattern from="^ABC-\d+" to="Equipment"/>
<Pattern from="^ABC-[0-9]{3}" to="Equipment"/>

</Patterns>

The resulting tag is "ABC-123".

Separator Attribute

The default separator used in concatenation can be changed using the separator

attribute as follows:

<Pattern from="[A-Z]-\d{3}" to="valve" separator="*"/>

For example, if the concatenated string is formed from "P" and "100", the resultant tag is

P*100.

Context Attribute

The default context used in the output EIWM file can be changed using the "context"

attribute as follows:

<Pattern from="[A-Z]-\d{3}" to="valve" context="xxx"/>

Objects matched by the pattern have context xxx rather than the default.

In order to have nested context in the output EIWM XML file, you can include the ‘|’

character. For example, when the default context is AAA|BBB and the pattern context is

CCC|DDD then the output EIWM XML file is as follows:

<Object>

<ID>C-9001</ID>
<Context>

<ID>DDD</ID>
<Context>

<ID>CCC</ID>
<Context>

<ID>BBB</ID>
<Context>

<ID>AAA</ID>

</Context>

</Context>

Release 5.0.11

Page 24

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

</Context>

</Context>
<ClassID>Equi</ClassID>
<Association type=”is referenced in”>
<Object>

<ID>C-9001_pdf</ID>
<Context>

<ID>BBB</ID>
<Context>

<ID>AAA</ID>

</Context>

</Context>

</Object>
</Association>

</Object>

In some special scenarios nested context at the document level instead at object level

may be required. To achieve this, use the ~ (tilt) sign.

For example, where the default context is ABC|~XYZ, then the output EIWM XML file is

as follows:

<Object>

<ID>C-9001</ID>
<Context>

<ID>ABC</ID>

</Context>
<ClassID>Equi</ClassID>
<Association type=”is referenced in”>
<Object>

<ID>C-9001_pdf</ID>
<Context>

<ID>XYZ</ID>
<Context>

<ID>ABC</ID>

</Context>

</Context>

</Object>
</Association>

</Object>

Excluding Patterns

It is possible, using the <Exclude> element, to set a pattern to exclude certain matches

found by the search pattern. In the following example, the text 'PID-1234' representing

a P&ID document name can be found by the search pattern [A-Z]{1,3}-\d{3,4}. If you

want to only identify tags and not P&ID names you can do this by using the exclude

pattern 'PID-'. The exclude value can be any regular expression search pattern.

Release 5.0.11

Page 25

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

Modifying Tags Found

As a tag is the ID of an item in AVEVA NET and tags may come from different sources that

may not be consistent in their naming or you need to enable for mistakes in tags, it is

possible to manipulate a tag once found and use the manipulated tag in the XML file(s)

written to the AVEVA NET staging area. For example, one application reports a tag as P-

100A whereas the same item may be referred to in another by P100A and in another

application as 'P-100A/B' or _-100A-C. For AVEVA NET it is essential to consolidate

these.

Expanding a Tag

One or more <Expand> sections specify expansion characters <Char> that when found

in a tag may result in that tag’s expansion. The following example states that both

'&' (represented in XML as &amp;) and '-' results in expansion if found in a sequence

represented by the corresponding pattern. For example, if a tag 'P-100A&B' is found in a

document then the search pattern '[A-Z]&amp;[A-Z]' is applied to it resulting in 'A&B'

which is then  split using the character '&' resulting in 'A' and 'B'. The Gateway then

reports 'P-100A' and 'P-100B' as being associated with the document.

Note: The 'P-100A&B' also contains a ‘-’ which can be an expansion character but as it is

not within a string matched by the associated that is, '[A-Z]-[A-Z]' then it is not used for

expansion for this tag. So the expansion character and the pattern (which must contain

the expansion character) allow the necessary flexibility for handling common tag formats.

Finally, it is possible for an <Expand> to set the attribute ‘Interpolate’ to

"true" (default is false) which results in the Gateway creating items implied within a tag

such as 'P-100A-D' that is, 'P-100A', 'P-100B' and so on. The interpolate feature is

restricted to the case where the expansion character separates two other characters.

<Expand>

<SubPattern>[A-Z]&amp;[A-Z]</SubPattern>
<Char>&amp;</Char>

</Expand>
<Expand Interpolate = "true">

<SubPattern>[A-Z]-[A-Z]</SubPattern>
<Char>-</Char>

</Expand>

Only the final occurrence of each pattern within is considered for expansion. So the tag ‘P-

A5-100A-B’ would not be expanded at both 'P-A' and 'A-B'. If such a scenario is possible

it is better to make the pattern only apply if at the end of a tag that is, <Pattern>[A-Z]-

[A-Z]$</Pattern> to avoid unintended expansions. You can expand tags for each

pattern in a mapping file.

Release 5.0.11

Page 26

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

For example:

<Pattern from="[A-Z]{1}\d{3}[A-Z]/[A-Z]" to="PipeLine" separator="*"
context="Hello">

<Expand Interpolate="true">

<SubPattern>[A-Z]/[A-Z]</SubPattern>
<Char>/</Char>

</Expand>

</Pattern>
<Pattern from="\d{2}[A-Z]{2}\d{3}[A-Z]-[A-Z]" to="PipeLine"
context="Hello">

<Expand Interpolate="true">

<SubPattern>[A-Z]-[A-Z]</SubPattern>
<Char>-</Char>

</Expand>

</Pattern>

Single Pattern with Multiple Expand Sections

If a pattern contains more than one Expand section, the patterns will be applied one after

the other and the resulting tags of each expansion become the input for the next Expand

section.

Example: P-101AA/BB/DD 456 AC/AD/AF

<Expand Interpolate="false">
    <SubPattern>[A-Z]{2}/[A-Z]{2}/[A-Z]{2}</SubPattern>
    <Char>/</Char>
</Expand>
<Expand Interpolate="false">
      <SubPattern>[A-Z]{2}/[A-Z]{2}/[A-Z]{2}</SubPattern>
      <Char>/</Char>
</Expand>

The first expand sub-pattern [A-Z]{2}/[A-Z]{2}/[A-Z]{2} is applied on the text P-

101AA/BB/DD 456 AC/AD/AF, from right to left. It first matches with AC/AD/AF and

generates the tags P-101AA/BB/DD 456 AC, P-101AA/BB/DD 456 AD, P-

101AA/BB/DD 456 AF. The second expand sub-pattern [A-Z]{2}/[A-Z]{2}/[A-Z]

{2} is applied to the tags that  were generated in the first expansion. It matches with

AA/BB/DD, which is then expanded to new tags: "P-101AA 456 AC", "P-101AA 456

AD", "P-101AA 456 AF", "P-101BB 456 AC" , "P-101BB 456 AD" , "P-101BB 456

AF" , "P-101DD 456 AC" ,"P-101DD 456 AD" and "P-101DD 456 AF".

Note: Interpolate on text values with more than one character is not supported.

Release 5.0.11

Page 27

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

Replacing Part of a Tag

The Replace section enables you to search for part of a tag using a regular expression and

replace it with either a sub-pattern of the original pattern or a fixed string value.

Notes:

· The replacement search pattern should be a subset of the original search pattern.
· If an original pattern is found more than once in a tag, then each occurrence is

replaced.

· You can define one or more <Replace> sections both within a <Pattern>

section as well as outside a <Pattern> section.

· If a <Replace> section is within a <Pattern> section, then it applies only to the

tags identified by the pattern.

· If a <Replace> section is outside any <Pattern> section, then it applies to all

the tags identified by all the patterns defined in the pattern mapping file.

The following example uses the <Replace> functionality to search part of a tag, using a

regular expression and then replace it with a sub-pattern.

<Pattern from=”[A-Z]-\d{3}" to="valve">
   <Replace>
      <Original>[A-Z]-</Original>
      <Replacement>[A-Z]</Replacement>
   </Replace>
</Pattern>

The above pattern mapping example can be used to find the tag 'P-100' and is then

transformed to 'P100’ using the <Replace> configuration.

· <Original> specifies the part of the tag that is of interest, for example 'P-'.
· <Replacement> defines what this part will be replaced by, either a pattern on
that part as in the example where 'P-' will be replaced by 'P', or a fixed string

value.

The following example uses the <Replace> functionality within the <Pattern> section

to search part of a tag using a regular expression and replace '-' with a fixed string value

'*'.

<Pattern from="[A-Z]-\d{3}" to="valve">
   <Replace>
      <Original>-</Original>
      <Replacement isRegex=”false”>*</Replacement>
   </Replace>
</Pattern>

The following example uses the <Replace> functionality within the <Pattern> section

to search part of a tag using a regular expression and replace '-' with a space value ' '.

Release 5.0.11

Page 28

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

<Pattern from="[A-Z]-\d{3}" to="valve">
   <Replace>
      <Original>-</Original>
      <Replacement xml:space="preserve" isRegex=”false”> </Replacement>
   </Replace>
</Pattern>

The following example uses the <Replace> functionality within the <Pattern> section

to search part of a tag using a regular expression and replace a space value ' ' with '-'.

<Pattern from="[A-Z] \d{3}" to="valve">
   <Replace>
      <Original xml:space="preserve"> </Original>
      <Replacement isRegex=”false”>-</Replacement>
   </Replace>
</Pattern>

The following example uses the <Replace> functionality within a <Pattern> section

along with Expand.

Note: When a pattern has defined both Expand and Replace functions, Expand is

applied first followed by Replace.

<Pattern from="[A-Z]{2}-\d{3}[A-Z]-[A-Z]" to="pump">
   <Expand Interpolate="true">
      <SubPattern>[A-Z]-[A-Z]</SubPattern>
      <Char>-</Char>
   </Expand>
   <Replace>
      <Original>[A-Z]-</Original>
      <Replacement>[A-Z]</Replacement>
   </Replace>
</Pattern>

The above pattern mapping example can be used to find the tag 'PU-001A-B', which is

then expanded to ‘PU-001A’ and ‘PU-001B’. The expanded tags are then transformed to

‘PU001A’ and ‘PU001B’ respectively using Replace.

The following code provides an example of using the <Replace> functionality within a

<Pattern> section along with Expand and Insert.

Note: When a pattern has defined Expand along with one or more Replace or Insert

functions, Expand is applied first and the resulting values are passed to all Replace and

Insert sections in the order listed in the configuration.

Release 5.0.11

Page 29

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

<Pattern from="[A-Z]{2}-\d{3}[A-Z]-[A-Z]" to="pump">
   <Expand Interpolate="true">
      <SubPattern>[A-Z]-[A-Z]</SubPattern>
      <Char>-</Char>
   </Expand>
   <Replace>
      <Original>[A-Z]-</Original>
      <Replacement>[A-Z]</Replacement>
   </Replace>
   <Insert position="after">
      <SubPattern>[A-Z]{2}</SubPattern>
      <String>-</String>
   </Insert>
</Pattern>

The above pattern mapping example can be used to find the tag 'PU-001A-B' which is

then expanded to 'PU-001A' and 'PU-001B'. The expanded tags are then transformed to

‘PU001A’ and ‘PU001B’ respectively using Replace. It can then be transformed to ‘PU-

001A’ and ‘PU-001B’ respectively using Insert.

The following example applies the <Replace> functionality multiple times within a

<Pattern> section, which can be used to modify the tags identified by a single pattern.

The <Replace> functionality also modifies the tags  outside the <Pattern> section

identified by all the patterns defined in the pattern mapping file.

<Pattern from="[A-Z]{1}-\d{3}-[A-Z]{2}" to="PipeLine">
   <Replace>
      <Original>[A-Z]-</Original>
      <Replacement>[A-Z]</Replacement>
   </Replace>
</Pattern>
<Pattern from="\d{2}-[A-Z]{2}#\d{3}-[A-Z]{2}" to="PipeLine">
   <Replace>
      <Original>[A-Z]#</Original>
      <Replacement>[A-Z]</Replacement>
   </Replace>
</Pattern>
<Replace>
      <Original>[0-9]-</Original>
      <Replacement>[0-9]</Replacement>
</Replace>

The above mapping examples can be used to find the tags 'P-001-AA' and '00-PU#001-

BB'. The tag 'P-001-AA' is transformed to 'P001-AA' and the tag '00-PU#001-BB' is

transformed to '00-PU001-BB' using the locally defined Replace. The tags 'P001-AA'

and '00-PU001-BB' are then transformed to 'P001AA' and '00PU001BB' using the

globally defined Replace that removes the '-' character.

Release 5.0.11

Page 30

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

Inserting a String into a Tag

One or more Insert sections enable the specification of patterns to search within a tag

and insert a string using a before/after pattern.

Notes:

· If the sub-pattern is found more than once in a tag, then the insertion is applied

on each occurrence.

· You can define one or more Insert sections both within a <Pattern> section,
where it applies only to the strings identified by the pattern, or outside all

<Pattern> sections, where it applies globally to all tags.

The following example enables "a 'P100' tag, which is found in a document, to be reported

as 'P-100' using the insert after function. The position attribute specifies whether the

string is added 'before' or ‘after’ the pattern (it defaults to after).

<Pattern from="[A-Z]\d{3}" to="pump">
   <Insert position="after">
      <SubPattern>[A-Z]</SubPattern>
      <String>-</String>
   </Insert>
</Pattern>

The following example can be used to transform a 'P100' tag into 'P-100' using insert

before.

<Pattern from="[A-Z]\d{3}" to="pump">
   <Insert position="before">
      <SubPattern>\d{3}</SubPattern>
      <String>-</String>
   </Insert>
</Pattern>

The following example uses the <Expand> and <Insert> functions within a <Pattern>

section.

Note: When a pattern has defined both Expand and Insert, the Expand is applied first

followed by Insert.

<Pattern from="[A-Z]{2}\d{3}[A-Z]-[A-Z]" to="pump">
   <Expand Interpolate="true">
      <SubPattern>[A-Z]-[A-Z]</SubPattern>
      <Char>-</Char>
   </Expand>
   <Insert position="before">
      <SubPattern>\d{3}</SubPattern>
      <String>-</String>
   </Insert>
</Pattern>

Release 5.0.11

Page 31

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

The above mapping can be used to expand the tag ‘PU001A-B’ to ‘PU001A’ and

‘PU001B’. The expanded tags are then transformed to ‘PU-001A’ and ‘PU-001B’ using

Insert.

The following example uses an <Insert> function within a <Pattern> section to modify

the tags identified by the pattern, and another <Insert> function outside the

<Pattern> to modify all the tags identified by all the patterns in the mapping file.

<Pattern from="[A-Z]{1}\d{3}[A-Z]{2}" to="PipeLine">
   <Insert position="before">
      <SubPattern>\d{3}</SubPattern>
      <String>-</String>
   </Insert>
</Pattern>
<Pattern from="\d{2}-[A-Z]{2}\d{3}[A-Z]{2}" to="PipeLine">
   <Insert position="after">
      <SubPattern>\d{2}-[A-Z]{2}</SubPattern>
      <String>-</String>
   </Insert>
</Pattern>
<Insert position="after">
   <SubPattern>\d{3}</SubPattern>
   <String>-</String>
</Insert>

This mapping could be used to find the tags ‘P001AA’ and ’00-PU001BB’. These  are

then transformed to ‘P-001AA’ and ’00-PU-001BB’ using the locally defined Insert. The

locally transformed tags are then transformed to ‘P-001-AA’ and ’00-PU-001-BB’  using

the globally defined Insert.

The order in which these functions are applied affect the final transformations:

· When an Expand function is used with one or more Replace/Insert functions, the
Expand is applied first and the resulting values are passed to all Replace and

Insert sections in the order listed in the configuration.

· All the tags identified and transformed within each Pattern section are then
transformed by any globally defined Replace settings followed by all globally

defined Insert settings.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

5.3.2

Settings

You can use the Settings tab under Documents to define the spreadsheet format, set

various file locator options, time outs and so on. The Settings tab is shown in the following

screenshot:

Spreadsheet Format

You can use the Document Type option under Spreadsheet Format group to specify the

type of Excel files (.xlsx) that ranges are to be applied to.or .xlsx) that ranges are to be

applied to. It is used in conjunction with the Use Ranges setting.

Some spreadsheets have graphical content as these cannot be extracted using IFilters.

Instead the Open XML SDK software from Microsoft can be used to extract those

spreadsheets. However, the Open XML SDK software works with only the Office 2010 .xlsx

files.

Release 5.0.11

Page 33

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

Use Ranges

You can use the Use Ranges option under Settings group to specify whether ranges are to

be used when hot-spotting Excel files.

Options

You can use the Options group to specify various folder location, file search patterns and

moving of the processed and unprocessed files in the Gateway.

Options

Description

Copy Other Files to
Staging Area

Copy Source Files to
Staging Area

Search File Names
for Tags

Create Trigger File

Move Processed And
Unprocessed Files

The Gateway processes files in the source folder of types
specified in its configuration file. However, other files in the
source folder may be required in the staging area.
Other files may be generated externally, for example,
folder.vnet, or files produced as a result of the processing
controlled by the Gateway, for example, SVG files. The
configuration file specifies which files are copied to the staging
area if this option is selected

Indicates that all source files are copied to the staging area.

If you select this box, then the file name (excluding the
extension) is checked for the patterns given in the pattern
mapping file. If there is a pattern match found for the file
name, then the file is processed. If there is no pattern match
found for the file name, then the file is not processed.
A message is added in the log file and the same message is
shown to you as an error while processing files once all the
files are processed.

If you select this option, it creates a trigger.start file in the
staging area folder. This enables the AVEVA NET Import
controller to automatically process files added to the staging
area folder.

If you select this box then the input files are either moved to
the Processed or Unprocessed folders as appropriate. By
default, this box is selected in which case the input files are
left in the source folder.
Note: When you click the Monitor button, the input files are
always moved to the Processed or Unprocessed folders
regardless of whether this box is selected or not.

Insert Line Breaks

If you select this option, it will insert line breaks while
converting word files to test files.

Object ID From VNet File If you select this option, Object ID is considered from the

VNet File located at the source folder of the project. If you do
not select this option, Object ID is taken as the input source
file name.

Release 5.0.11

Page 34

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

Time Outs

You can use the Time Outs settings in the Configuration settings to specify the time taken

by the Gateway in processing a file within a specific time.

Timeout Settings

Description

Open File Time Out Specifies whether or not the application “times out” processing a

file if it takes as long as the number of seconds entered in the field.
This may be useful in preventing the application taking too long
attempting to process corrupted files.
If a time out occurs, unprocessed input files are moved to the
Unprocessed folder.

Activate Processing Time Out

Specifies whether or not the application “times out” if total time of
a processing operating takes as long as the number of minutes
entered in the field. Fractions of minutes may be entered, for
example, 0.5 minutes = 30 seconds. If a time out occurs,
unprocessed input files are moved to the Unprocessed folder.

ALS Connection

Retry Time

(Optional) Specifies the timeout period for the Gateway to wait for a
response from the ALS. When the Gateway requests a license from
the ALS and does not receive a response, for example, if the ALS
server or network is busy, the Gateway process waits for the
timeout period and then sends another license request to the ALS.

If a license is acquired, the Gateway processing starts. Otherwise,
the Gateway stops processing and reports the appropriate license
error. The timeout period can be configured to have values between
0 and 4294967295 seconds.

For example, set the ALS connection Retry Timeout to 120 s and run
the Gateway. If the ALS server is unavailable, the following pop up
message appears:

When you click OK, the pop-up message box closes and the request
waits for 120 s for the ALS server to become available.

Release 5.0.11

Page 35

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

Timeout Settings

Description

· If the server is unavailable after 120 s, the Gateway raises a

respective license error.

· If the server is available within 120 s, the Gateway proceeds

with the execution of files.

When you click Cancel, it closes the pop-up box. It stops the
execution of files immediately and raises a valid license error.

Notes:

· You should expect a delay of 10-40 s for a normal ALS

response.

· The Gateway does not wait for the timeout period to expire
if the configured ALS connection details are invalid or the

ALS responds with a license checkout error.

Conversion

The older .xls files have to be converted to .xlsx format in order to be processed. The

Gateway can process these .xls source files one after another by selecting the Check to

convert .xls Source Files to .xlsx before processing box on the Settings tab.

Note: This conversion process slows down the Gateway slightly.

The Conversion group has the following two settings:

Conversion setting

Description

Check to convert .doc Source Files to .docx before processing Specifies whether .doc

Check to convert .xls Source Files to .xlsx before processing

files are to be converted
to .docx format before
processing.

Specifies whether .xls
files are to be converted
to .xlsx format before
processing.

Note: An alternative method is to perform a bulk conversion of xls files to xlsx files

before using the Gateway. Such conversion utilities can be found on the Internet. For

example:

· http://download.cnet.com/Batch-XLSX-to-XLS-Converter/3000-2077_4-

10910569.html

· http://www.labnol.org/software/bulk-convert-office-documents-in-2007-

format/4291/ (search for "bulk convert xls to xlsx")

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Working with the Gateway Dialog

5.4

Run the Gateway from the User Inteface

To run the Gateway:

1.

Click the Run button or click the Monitor button.

A Processing dialog is then displayed, showing the progress of the generation

process.

2.

Click Cancel or X to stop the process at any time.

Separate log files are created for each input file. These log files include error

information.

View Report:

To get the View Report:

1.

Click View Report.

The Gateway Summary Report is displayed summarising the results of output

file generation procedures for the project and access of the project file, file

generation log file and so on.

5.5

Monitor the Gateway

To monitor the Gateway:

1.

Click Monitor.

The Monitor form is opened and the process is initiated after you click Start.

The form displays the progress of the process. It also lists each file as it is found

and converted.

2.

Click Stop to stop the process at any time or select the Stop option from the

File menu.

When you stop the process, or when you close the form after the processing is

complete, the Monitoring Process Completed message is displayed.

Release 5.0.11

Page 37

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 6 - Extracting Text from Files

6

Extracting Text from Files

The Gateway extracts the text from document files so that this text can then be searched

for tags. That text can be extracted from a particular file type using the following ways:

· IFilters (preferred method) or Jet OLEDB technology (for Excel spreadsheets)
· AutoDesk DWG files
· An external application

For more information about associating an IFilter or external application with a particular

file type, see Using the Configuration file.

6.1

IFilters or Jet OLEDB Technology

IFilters is a Microsoft technology used by Windows Indexing Service among other things to

search the textual content of files. The Gateway uses these to extract the text from a file.

Windows have IFilters built in for common Microsoft applications such as Word and

PowerPoint. For Microsoft Excel spreadsheets, the Jet OLEDB technology is used instead.

6.2

AutoDesk DWG Files

Processing of .dwg files requires an IFilter. AVEVA does not supply such an IFilter.

However, various vendors of design software that produce .dwg exports often provide

them.

If you install this IFilter, .dwg files can be processed. If you do not install .dwg IFilter then

a "no IFilter found" error message is logged.

Release 5.0.11

Page 38

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 6 - Extracting Text from Files

6.3

External Applications

Although IFilters are the preferred methods to extract text from document files, yet not all

file types have IFilters. The Gateway can drive external applications to extract the textual

content from document files.

Note: Applications that can only be driven from a command line and that produce a

textual output file can be used as an external application.

Release 5.0.11

Page 39

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 7 - Using the Configuration File

7

Using the Configuration File

The Gateway uses configuration files to store the selected settings using the user interface

(for example, Source folder) and other option such as by editing the configuration files.

You can edit the configuration files (in XML format) using an XML Editor and a Notepad or

any text editor. Any number of configuration files may exist, typically one per project. You

can select which option is to be used by opening the File, Select Configuration menu.

A number of settings that the application uses are not available through the user

interface. To edit these settings the configuration file can be edited directly in a text or

XML editor. These settings are explained below.

7.1

Input File Types

You can use the configuration file section <InputFiles> to define which file types are

considered by the Gateway if they are found in the source folder. One <File> section for

each file type/extension is considered that defines how files of that type is processed.

For file types with an IFilter available (the Gateway automatically finds the correct IFilter if

it is available):

<File>

<Extension>pdf</Extension>
<Type>IndexFilter</Type>

</File>
<File>

<Extension>doc</Extension>
<Type>IndexFilter</Type>

</File>
<File>

<Extension>xls</Extension>
<Type>IndexFilter</Type>

</File>

For a zip file the configuration section would resemble:

<File>

<Extension>zip</Extension>
<Type>Zip</Type>

</File>

In the case of zip files, the Gateway unzips the file (to a temporary directory that gets

deleted) then searches the files within the zip file and generates an AVEVA NET Workhub

and Dashboard xml file relating tags found to the zip file. Only those files within the zip

file that have a corresponding section in the configuration file are searched for tags.

Release 5.0.11

Page 40

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 7 - Using the Configuration File

7.2

Log Folder

The Log Folder parameter defines the location of the log files where a new folder is

created for each file type. The log file for each specific file type is placed in its relevant

folder.

<LogFolder>C:\Test123\Logs</LogFolder>

7.3

Document ID

On searching a file, the Gateway creates associations between tags and the related file

extension. So if you search mydoc.doc then the document ID is ‘mydoc’. However, you

can use only part of the file name for the ID, for example, there may be a version number

as part of the file name that you may not want.

Within the configuration file you can add a setting:

<DocumentIdSearchPattern>[A-Z]{3}</DocumentIdSearchPattern>

The above setting specifies a regular expression that is applied to the file name (after the

extension has been removed) to find the Document ID used in the output XML file. If the

search returns nothing then no output is generated for that document.

Note: If it is run from the command line (see Running the Gateway from the Command

Line) with the ‘–r’ setting, then any DocumentIdSearchPattern setting is ignored.

7.4

Document Revision

The Gateway can extract the document revision if it is included as part of the document’s

file name. This is achieved by applying a regular expression pattern to the file name

(minus its extension).

For example, if the file name is ‘mydoc v2.doc’ indicating a revision of ‘2’ then the

following setting can be included in the configuration file (‘$’ indicates only to consider

numbers found at the end of the file name):

<RevisionSearchPattern>\d{1,}$</RevisionSearchPattern>

which indicates any numbers found at the end of the file name is the document revision:

<Object>

<ID>mydoc v2</ID>
<Context>

<ID>Test</ID>

</Context>
<Revision>2</Revision>

</Object>

Release 5.0.11

Page 41

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 7 - Using the Configuration File

If a revision search pattern is specified and no revision is found for a file name then no

output is generated for that file. If you want to specify a revision search pattern but want

output for files for which no revision is found, you can use a pattern such as:

<RevisionSearchPattern>(\d{1,}$|$)</RevisionSearchPattern>

which means that the either the revision pattern ‘\d{1,}$’ or the end of the string ‘$’ is

searched for. As a string always has an end something is found for the revision even if it is

an empty string – in which case the revision line is omitted from the output file.

7.5

Temporary Directory

When processing zip files the Gateway unzips the file to a temporary location. If no

temporary directory is specified in the configuration file then the files are unzipped to

your Windows application folder, for example, ‘C:\Documents and Settings\user

name\ApplicationData’.

You can use a different folder for disk space or other reasons and this can be done by

specifying a temporary directory in the configuration file:

<TempFolder>C:\temp</TempFolder>

This is only relevant if you process the zip files.

7.6

Copy Other Files to Staging Area

The user interface option Copy Other Files to Staging Area is detailed in Working with the

Gateway Dialog. The configuration section <CopyOtherFilesToStaging> lists the document

types that are copied to the staging area if found in the source folder.

<CopyOtherFilesToStaging Perform="true">

<Extension>vnet</Extension>

</CopyOtherFilesToStaging>

The copy is performed after all processing is carried out so files being moved can be those

created during processing – though any move to set for a file type specified in the Input

Files section takes precedence.

7.7

Copy Source Files to Staging Area

The Copy Source Files to Staging Area user interface option is detailed in Working with

the Gateway Dialog. In the configuration file, this option is found in the <InputFiles>

section:

<InputFiles Copy="true">

Release 5.0.11

Page 42

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 7 - Using the Configuration File

7.8

Move Input Files to Processed and Unprocessed

The MoveProcessedAndUnProcessedFiles user interface option is detailed in Working

with the Gateway Dialog. In the configuration file, this option is found in the

<MoveProcessedAndUnprocessedFiles> section:

<MoveProcessedAndUnprocessedFiles>false
</MoveProcessedAndUnprocessedFiles>

7.9

OpenFileTimeOut

The Open File Time Out user interface option is detailed in Working with the Gateway

Dialog. In the configuration file, these options are found in the <OpenFileTimeOut>

section:

<OpenFileTimeOut Perform="true">

<OpenFileTimeOutSeconds>10</OpenFileTimeOutSeconds>

</OpenFileTimeOut>

7.10

Convert .doc to .docx

The Check to Convert .doc Source Files to .docx before processing user interface option is

detailed in Working with the Gateway Dialog. In the configuration file, this option is found

in the <ConvertDocToDocx> section:

<ConvertDocToDocx Perform="false"></ConvertDocToDocx>

7.11

Convert .xls to .xlsx

The Check to Convert .xls Source Files to .xlsx before processing user interface option is

detailed in Working with the Gateway Dialog. In the configuration file, this option is found

in the <ConvertXlsToXlsx> section:

<ConvertXlsToXlsx Perform="false"></ConvertXlsToXlsx>

Release 5.0.11

Page 43

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 7 - Using the Configuration File

7.12

Fix File Names

The configuration file section <FixFileNames> contains a number of <Replace>

sections each of which defines a string, identified by Original XML, that is considered to

be invalid if found in a file or folder name. Each <Original> has a corresponding

Replacement string that may be used to replace the invalid string in the file name.

<FixFileNames>
<Replace>

<Original xml:space="preserve"> </Original>
<Replacement xml:space="preserve"> </Replacement>

</Replace>
<Replace>
…

</Replace>
</FixFileNames>

The above example indicates that a double space is invalid. If you choose to rectify this

then a single space is used as a replacement for all files and subfolders within the source

folder.

Note: The attribute <xml:space="preserve"> is essential if the original or replacement

strings have leading or trailing spaces. Without this attribute setting, such space is not

considered in the search and replace procedure.

7.13

Non-Standard Associations

You can use the configuration file section <Association> to change the association

settings within the target <file name>_null.xml file. Currently, the only option is for

changing the name of the association from "is referenced in" to another setting.

For example:

<Association>
<Type>

<Description>has document</Description>

</Type>
</Association>

produces a target <file name>_null.xml file with the Association type="has

document" as follows:

<Association type="has document">

<Object>

<ID>Training Line List</ID>

<Context>

<ID>Test</ID>

</Context>

</Object>

</Association>

Release 5.0.11

Page 44

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 8 - Running the Gateway from the Command Line

8

Running the Gateway from the Command Line

You can run the Gateway from the command line. You can map to a folder not just a

specific file. You have to use the following setting to map to the source folder/file for the

Gateway:

“C:\...\AVEVA.NET.Document.Indexing.Gateway.exe” –c
“c:\..\myconfig.xml” –f “c:\..\myfile.doc”

Setting

Parameter
Type

Description

-c “c:\..\myconfig.xml”  Mandatory  Specifies the configuration file to use as the

-f “c:\..\myfile.doc”
or
-f "c:\..\source"

Optional

-r "mydoc"

Optional

basis of the run.

A single file or a directory must be specified as
the input. When the Gateway is run in
command line mode, the source directory
setting in the configuration file is overridden by
command arguments as this setting is expected
to change often.

By default, the Gateway creates associations
between a tag and a document, where the
document object name is the input document
file name. When processing a single file, this
parameter can specify a different name for the
output document. For example, the input file is
called ‘mydoc v1.doc’, but the output
document to be imported into AVEVA NET must
be ‘mydoc’. The output EIWM file's name and
the document object ID that is referenced from
within it, are also changed. This will ensure
consistent associations between tags in the
EIWM and the document in AVEVA NET that
refers to them.

-d

Optional  When set this option deletes the destination

and staging area files that results for processing
the file specified by the –f setting. This enables
you to capture the deletion of a document if you
have an application driving the Gateway
through the command line. The file specified by
–f need not exist as the expectation is that it
has been deleted.

Specifies an alternative target folder for the
staging area.

-t “c:\..\Staging Area”  Optional

-s

Optional

Silent Mode. When set, message boxes are not
displayed.

Release 5.0.11

Page 45

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA NET Document Indexing Gateway User Guide

CHAPTER 8 - Running the Gateway from the Command Line

Setting

-noReport

Parameter
Type

Description

Optional  When this option is used, the application does
not create the summary.log file or
report.mhtml. When this option is not used,
the application creates these files.

-context "IPE|AreaA|
AssetC"

Optional

Specifies the default context value (can be
nested using the pipe character) to be used in
processing the file. This command line
argument takes precedence over any default
context specified in the configuration file.

Exit Codes

The following command line exit codes are defined for

AVEVA.NET.Document.Indexing.Gateway.exe.

Exit Codes

Definition

0

1

1001

1002

1003

1004

Success

Failure: this indicates at least something or possibly everything

License checkout failure due to expiry of license

License checkout failure due to less number of seats

License for the product is missing or not requested

Invalid License server details/unavailable

Note: To read the error code returned from the Gateway, you can use the %errorlevel%

variable in batch mode. The variable contains the error code as mentioned in the above

table.

AVEVA Information Standards Manager Importing from MS Excel Guide

CHAPTER 1 - Preface

1

Preface

This chapter gives basic information about this guide, training and support.

1.1

About this Guide

This guide describes the requirements set by AVEVA Information Standards Manager™

(AVEVA ISM) while reading Class Library definitions from a Microsoft® Excel® (Excel or MS

Excel) workbook, according to ISM Standard Excel Format v1.2.0.

This document is accompanied by ISM Standard Excel Format v1.2.0.xlsx, which is an

Excel workbook and contains examples of each of the sheets explained here.

When loading Class Library content from an Excel workbook, AVEVA ISM can load the

content into an existing Class Library model. Alternatively, you can create a new model

based on the workbook content. You may also load multiple workbooks, one at a time,

into the same resulting Class Library model.

1.5

Technical Support

If you encounter software issues or have suggestions for software improvements, contact

the AVEVA Helpdesk (https://avevahelpdesk.aveva.com), and register a support incident.

After you register a support incident, we know your identity (from your customer ID) and

the details of the incident. We endeavour to fix the software issue or consider the

software improvement suggestion as soon as possible, and notify you about the status of

the incident.

If you are an accredited user of the AVEVA products, you are entitled to use the AVEVA

Helpdesk, which you should have valid login credentials. For any other queries, contact

the nearest AVEVA Regional Support Centre (http://support.aveva.com/support).

CHAPTER 2 - Rules for Excel Workbook

2

Rules for Excel Workbook

· The class library content has to be provided in a Microsoft Excel 2007-2013

(*.xslx) workbook format. The workbook may be provided as a single file in a zip

archive.

· Sheet names and column headers (row 1) are not case sensitive.
· The reader will look for sheet names that it recognises, and recognised column

headers in those sheets, to extract the class library content.

· All sheets are optional, but if some are missing the resulting Class Library model

might not constitute a consistent Class Library model.

· Sheet names, or column names, that are not recognised will be ignored.
· The sheets might be distributed over multiple workbooks, which can be loaded in

sequence to extend the same Class Library model instance.

· Required column headers that cannot be found will be reported on.
· Rows not containing a value in a required column will be reported on.
· Some columns may be constrained to only contain values in certain ranges (for
example, Y/N, or true/false). Violation of such constraints will be reported on.

· Rows containing empty cells only will be ignored.
· Empty cells will be ignored, unless it represents a required value.
· When loading a workbook into an existing Class Library model, cells containing

the text {null} will clear the corresponding property in the resulting Class Library

model.

· The order of the sheets in the workbook, and the order of the columns in a

sheet, are not  significant.

· All rows in all sheets might include a column _ACTION, where certain values will

have specific meaning:

Ignore: The corresponding spread sheet row will be ignored.

· Note: The corresponding spread sheet row is considered to contain a note,

and will therefore be ignored.

· Delete: When loading into an existing Class Library model, the definition

corresponding to current row will be deleted. If no such definition exists, the

row will be ignored.

· Other values in this column will be reported on.

Release 2.2

Page 8

CHAPTER 3 - Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA ISM Intrinsic Definitions

3

Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA ISM Intrinsic
Definitions

Explanation to the column headers in the sections to follow:

· Column Name: Describes the name of a column header in the corresponding

spread sheet. The names are case insensitive, but they have to be spelled exactly

as stated (including spaces and punctuations).

· Constraint:

· Column Header: Means that the column header has to be in the spread
sheet, but the rows does not necessarily have to contain a value for the

column.

· Required: Means that the column header has to be in the spread sheet, and

all rows will have to contain a valid value for the column.

Italic Text: Represents a valid value for the column

· Multi-lingual definitions: Most of the definitions in the Class Library contain the

properties Name and Description. The values provided for these properties

should be in the default language (of your choice) of the Class Library. In addition

you can add arbitrary columns in the spread sheets for providing Name and

Description in additional languages. These column headers should be named

“Name {language}” and “Description {language}”, where {language} is composed

as “{languageCode}-{countryCode}”. For example, Name in Norwegian should

have the column header “Name nb-NO”

· The first argument is a valid ISO Language Code. These codes are the lower-

case two-letter codes as defined by ISO-639-1

· The second argument is a valid ISO Country Code. These codes are the

upper-case two-letter codes as defined by ISO-3166

3.1

Sheet: 'ISM Extension Namespaces'

Every definition in a Class Library, such as Attributes, Functional Classes, Enumerations,

support a predefined set of intrinsic properties, such as Id, Name and Description. You

may extend any of these definitions with arbitrary properties of our choice. To do so, you

will first have to define an extension namespace and then associate your arbitrary

properties with this namespace. This sheet defines extension namespaces.

Release 2.2

Page 9

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

·
AVEVA Information Standards Manager Importing from MS Excel Guide

CHAPTER 3 - Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA ISM Intrinsic Definitions

Item Column Name Constraint Description

1

Prefix

Required

· A name of your choice to use as a prefix for arbitrary

extension properties (see below)
The value has to be a valid Xml no-colon name (NCName),
which basically means: it cannot start with a digit; it should
not contain spaces or colons.

2

Uri

Required

· A universal resource identifier, which is basically a string

that you can trust to be universally unique. A common way
to compose uris is to use a url, containing your companies
domain name, such as:

http://schemas.mydomain.com/classlibrary/extensions/
{topic}

where you replace {topic} with the purpose/target of the
extension namespace

3

4

5

Name

· A human friendly name of the namespace

Description

Type

- Generic
- Custom

This is the name that will be displayed in AVEVA ISM when
you work with extensions

· A description of the purpose of the namespace

Specifies the type of the namespace
o Generic: Extensions belonging to a namespace of this
type will be exposed for generic viewing and editing in
the AVEVA ISM user interface. This is the default type.
o Custom:  Extensions belonging to a namespace of this

type will not be exposed in the AVEVA ISM generic user
interface. This type is mainly intended for future versions,
when AVEVA ISM will support custom extensions to the
user interface.

You can add extension properties to any of the spread sheets described in this chapter, by

adding columns headers according to the syntax:

{prefix}:[Node1/…/NodeN/]{propertyName}

Assume you have defined a namespace (with prefix mapping) for mapping attribute

names to nomenclatures used by different software systems or industry standards. Then

you would typically extend the sheet ISM Attributes with column headers like:

· mapping:WorkMate/name
· mapping:Maximo/name
· mapping:ISO15926/name

Rules and impact of extensions are dictated by the intention of the target extension

namespace. In most cases this comes down to how the extensions are being adopted by

the consuming software. To be on the safe side, you should assume that extension node

and property names are case sensitive.

Release 2.2

AVEVA Information Standards Manager Importing from MS Excel Guide

CHAPTER 3 - Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA ISM Intrinsic Definitions

If a definition (for example, a Functional Class) is extended by another definition, any

extensions defined on the ancestor will by default be derived by the descendants. By

suffixing an extension property name by exclamation mark (for example,

mapping:ISO15926/name!), you qualify the property to not be derivable, and it will not

be derived by descendant definitions.

3.2

Sheet: 'ISM Class Library Header'

This sheet defines properties of the Class Library instance as a whole

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

1

Id

Required

The value to use as the unique identifier for the Class
Library definition

· When setting up inheritance between Class Library

instances, the identifier is used as reference
To avoid potential collisions of identifier values, when,
for example, the Class Library is stored in a common
repository, it is recommended to include your
company’s domain name, such as
“CorporateStandard@mydomain.com”
For a concrete, Asset specific, Class Library definition
(see Abstract below), it is recommended to compose
the Id as “Asset@mydomain.com”, where Asset
represents the code value used to identify the Asset in
instance data

The name of the target Class Library, typically a bit
more verbose and more human friendly than the
identifier value.

The version number consists of up to 4 segments of
integer numbers on the form
“major.minor.revision.build”, for example “1.0.1.1”
You only have to specify the number of segments that
you find applicable, the reminding segments will
automatically be set to 0

The date when the Class Library content was submitted
If specified as text, the value has to be on the form
“"yyyy.MM.dd” (four digits for year, two digits for
month and two digits for day of month)

· A reference to the Id of another Class Library to derive

from
Syntax is “id[, versionRequirements]” where
o id: Required, represents the Id value of the parent

Class Library

Description

· A description of the target Class Library

Version

Required

Name

2

3

4

5

VersionDate

6

Extends

AVEVA Information Standards Manager Importing from MS Excel Guide

CHAPTER 3 - Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA ISM Intrinsic Definitions

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

7

Abstract

- true
- false

o versionRequirements: Optional, and if not specified
the highest available version number will be used. If
specified, you can use wildcard, such as “1.2.*”,
meaning version number has to start on “1.2.”, but
the reminding two segments in the version number
are not significant

· A deployable Class Library will typically be a

composition of multiple definitions (or fragments), such
as:
o A corporate standard, containing the foundation of

attributes and class definitions

o A collection of naming templates, reflecting the

target ENS

o Application specific annotations, configuring

consuming software applications

o Industry standard and nomenclature mappings,
making the Class Library compliant with other
standards

Separately none of these fragments will constitute a
consistent Class Library for consumption, but combined
they will. Hence they should be annotated with
Abstract=true
Typically a deployable Class Library is targeting a
specific Asset (or Plant), covering requirements specific
to the target Asset. The Class Library fragment
representing these Asset specific requirements will
include all the other fragments as a composition, and
should be considered a concrete definition
(Abstract=false)
This setting will dictate certain behaviours of AVEVA
ISM:
o A fragment can only extend another fragment
o A fragment cannot be checked for consistency by

the AVEVA ISM consistency validators

o A fragment cannot be used for generating software

configuration artefacts in AVEVA ISM

3.3

Sheet: 'ISM Attributes'

This sheet defines Attribute (or Characteristic) classes.

Item Column Name

Constraint

Description

1

Id

Required

The identifier for the Attribute definition. There is a
requirement that this value is unique within the
collection of attributes in the target Class Library model

· Even though it is not an absolute requirement, it is
recommended that identifier values do not contain
white space characters

5

Name

Name
{language}

Description

Description
{language}

To avoid identifier naming conflicts in applications
implementing the Class Library it is recommended that
all identifiers, across all concepts, are unique within a
Class Library. This can easily be achieved by using a
naming convention, such as “ATT_nnnn”
It is strongly recommended, when a Class Library
evolves over time, to never change the value of any
identifiers

· A human readable name for the Attribute definition
· A human readable name for the Attribute definition, in

a specific language
The value for {language} is composed as
{languageCode}-{countryCode} (see the start of this
section)
This column can be repeated as many times as required,
each with a different value for {language}

· An arbitrary description of the purpose/role of the

Attribute

· An arbitrary description of the purpose/role of the

Attribute, in a specific language
The value for {language} is composed as
{languageCode}-{countryCode} (see the start of this
section)
This column can be repeated as many times as required,
each with a different value for {language}

6

Presence

- Not

· Dictates the requirements for a value being provided for

Applicable

- Optional
- Preferred
- Required

the Attribute when a data object is provided. For
example, when data is loaded by AVEVA NET
Gatekeeper, the constraint dictated by this property will
be enforced for all instances of the target Attribute

7

8

9

Presence
{maturity}

Discipline

Discipline
{maturity}

10

GroupId

Specifies the Presence requirement for the attribute
when a data object has a maturity of {maturity}. The
value for {maturity} should correspond to the identifier
of a Maturity Level definition
This column can be repeated as many times as required,
each with a different value for {maturity}

The discipline “owning“ the Attribute

The discipline “owning” the Attribute when a data
object has a maturity of {maturity}. The value for

Maturity Level definition
This column can be repeated as many times as required,
each with a different value for {maturity}

The name of the group that the Attribute belongs to
· Groups are created automatically, based on the values

provided in this column

- Enumeration

Inclusive

· An

Enumeration@aspect=”Enumerations.AttributeGroups”
will be created automatically, and populated with the
values supplied in this column

Specifies the minimum number of values that has to be
provided for the Attribute when an instance of a class is
provided. For example, when data is loaded by AVEVA
NET Gatekeeper, the constraint dictated by this
property will be enforced for all instances of the target
Attribute

Specifies the maximum number of values that can to be
provided for the Attribute when an instance of a class is
provided. For example, when data is loaded by AVEVA
NET Gatekeeper, the constraint dictated by this
property will be enforced for all instances of the target
Attribute

Specifies the identifier of a Unit Of Measure class
Supplying a value for this column will force
UomRequired=true and the DataType=Decimal

· When UomRequired=true there will be a requirement
for providing a UoM with instance values for the
Attribute. When editing instance values such Attributes
in a UI a drop-down list of applicable Unit Of Measure
Classes and/or Units Of Measures will be provided

· When “true” there will be a requirement for providing a

UoM with instance values for the Attribute. When
editing instance values such Attributes in a UI a drop-
down list of applicable Unit Of Measure Classes and/or
Units Of Measures will be provided

· When “true” will force DataType=Decimal

Specifies a constraint for how instance values for the
Attribute should be validated

· Column ValidationRule (see below) should then contain

Specifies the rule corresponding to what has been
specified for column ValidationType (see above):
o Enumeration:  the identifier for a corresponding
Enumeration list defined elsewhere in the Class
Library. When the instance values for the Attribute is
edited in a UI, the list of valid values will be provided
as a drop-down list

o RegularExpression: A Regular Expression syntax
constraining what values will be accepted for the
corresponding Attribute
