CHAPTER 3 - Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA ISM Intrinsic Definitions

Item Column Name

Constraint

Description

o ValueRangeInclusive: Two numeric values, separated
by comma. The first value sets the lower bound, and
the second value sets the upper bound for values
supplied for the Attribute

o Association: The validation rule Association indicates
that the corresponding attribute value represents the
identifier of another object of a given class. The rule
syntax is:

{concept}:{classId}[|{classId}|…|{classId}]

{concept} can be one of the following values:

Functional

· Physical
· Document
· General

{classId} is an identifier of a class of {concept}. The
square brackets in the syntax example above is
indicating that you optionally can specify more than
one {classId}. You should not include the square
brackets in actual syntax.

17

Concept

- Functional
- Document

· A value describing what concept(s) the Attribute is

intended for. Valid values are:
o Functional: The Attribute is intended for Functional

18

DataType

19

Size

- String
- Boolean
- Integer
- Decimal

classes

o Document: Then Attribute is intended for Document

classes

It is possible to specify more than one concept, in which
case the values need to be separated by a single space
character

· Dictates what data type the Attribute value has to

conform to
If a value is provided for column UomClassId, or
UomRequired (see above), the data type will be set to
Decimal, independent of what is specified in this column
· An expression specifying size restriction for the value of

the Attribute. Interpretation of the expression is
dependent on the consuming application, but the
following notation is recommended, depending on the
DataType of the Attribute:
o String:  A positive integer value expressing the

maximum number of characters that the
corresponding Attribute value can hold

o Integer: A positive integer value expressing the

maximum number of digits that the corresponding
Attribute value can hold

Item Column Name

21

Obsolete

- true
- false

22

SortOrder

- A signed
integer
value

o Decimal: Two positive integer values, separated by

comma, expressing maximum number of digits for the
integer part and the fractional part of the decimal
number that the corresponding Attribute value can
hold

· Annotating an Attribute with an aspect is a way to
associate the Attribute with a specific semantic
definition. Application logic will look for these aspects,
and apply associated processing rules. Definition of
available aspect values is outside the scope of this
document

· When evolving a Class Library over time, removing

previously existing definitions might cause issues with
consuming software systems (for example, they might
not support deleting an Attribute that is in use for
existing data). Obsoleting deprecated definitions might
therefore be a less intrusive approach. How, or whether,
obsoleting is implemented might vary across consuming
software though

· Decides on the order in which Attributes are presented

in the AVEVA ISM user interface

· Attributes not having a value specified for this property
will be ordered as if they have the highest SortOrder
value

· Consuming software systems might also utilise this

information to decide on in which order Attribute values
are to be presented

3.4

Sheet: 'ISM Functional Classes'

This sheet defines Functional Classes and Sub Classes (or Tag Classes).

An abstract class, “Root Functional Class” (@id=“ISM_ROOT_FUNCTIONAL”), will be

created automatically as the root of the class hierarchy, with all top level classes (classes
with no “Extends”) extending this class. You are free to define properties for this root

class, by including a class definition with Id=” ISM_ROOT_ FUNCTIONAL” in this sheet.

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

1

Id

Required

The identifier for the Class definition. There is a
requirement that this value is unique within the
collection of Functional Classes in the target Class Library
model

· Even though it is not an absolute requirement, it is

recommended that identifier values do not contain white
space characters.

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

2

3

4

5

Name

Name
{language}

Description

Description
{language}

6

Abstract

- true
- false

To avoid identifier naming conflicts in applications
implementing the Class Library it is recommended that all
identifiers, across all concepts, are unique within a Class
Library. This can easily be achieved by using a naming
convention, such as “TAG_nnnn”.
It is strongly recommended, when a Class Library evolves
over time, to never change the value of any identifiers.

· A human readable name for the Class definition.
· A human readable name for the Class definition, in a

specific language.
The value for {language} is composed as {languageCode}-
{countryCode} (see the start of this section).
This column can be repeated as many times as required,
each with a different value for {language}

· An arbitrary description for the purpose/role of the Class.
· An arbitrary description of the purpose/role of the Class,

in a specific language.
The value for {language} is composed as {languageCode}-
{countryCode} (see the start of this section).
This column can be repeated as many times as required,
each with a different value for {language}

Specifies whether the Class should be considered
Abstract.

· Abstract classes are typically base definitions for more
specialised concrete classes, like the way a Functional
Class forms a base for more specialised Sub Classes.

7

Discipline

The discipline “owning“ the Class (or Tag Discipline).

· An

Naming
Template_
ApplicableFor

- Validation

Enumeration@aspect=”Enumerations.FunctionalTypes”
will be created automatically, and populated with the

The value of this column should contain the identifier of
a Naming Template definition to associate with the Class
(see Naming Template definitions).
If you need to associate more than one Naming Template
to a Class, you have to use the Functional Class
Naming Templates sheet.

Specifies the applicability of the Naming Template, for
example, a Naming Template containing pattern
matching regular expressions will only be applicable for
Validation.
It is possible to specify more than one value, in which
case the values need to be separated by a single space
character.

LifeCycleType

· A reference to the identifier of a Life Cycle Type that is

applicable for the Class.

The identifier of the Functional Class being parent of
target Class. For example, when defining a Functional Sub
Class, this column typically contains the identifier of the
parent Functional Class.

· When extending a parent class, all aspects (properties,
attributes, naming templates, extensions) of the parent
class will automatically be derived by the target class.
Any explicit definitions on the target class will override
corresponding values being derived from parent class.
· Annotating a Class with an aspect is a way to associate
the Class with a specific semantic definition. Application
logic will look for these aspects, and apply associated
processing rules. Definition of available aspect values is
outside the scope of this document.

· When evolving a Class Library over time, removing

previously existing definitions might cause issues with
consuming software systems (for example, they might
not support deleting a class that is in use for existing
data). Obsoleting deprecated definitions might therefore
be a less intrusive approach. How, or whether, obsoleting
is implemented might vary across consuming software
though.

14

SortOrder

- A signed
integer
value

· Decides on the order in which classes are presented in

the AVEVA ISM user interface.

· Classes not having a value specified for this property will
be ordered as if they have the highest SortOrder value.

3.5

Sheet: 'ISM Functional Class Attributes'

This sheet defines association of Permissible Attributes (or Characteristics) with

Functional Classes.

If you have Permissible Attributes common to all Functional Classes, you can specify them

as permissible on a class with Id=”ISM_ROOT_ FUNCTIONAL” (see ISM Functional Classes),

and they will automatically be derived as permissible on all Functional Classes.

Description

1

2

Class_Id

Required

Id

Required

The identifier of the target Functional Class, as
defined in sheet ISM Functional Classes

The identifier of an Attribute, as defined in
sheet ISM Attributes, to be made permissible
for the target Functional Class
The values for the remaining columns is
automatically derived from this Attribute
definition, and can be overridden

3.7

Sheet: 'ISM Physical Classes'

This sheet defines Physical Classes and Sub Classes (or Equipment Classes).

An abstract class, “Root Physical Class” (@id=“ISM_ROOT_PHYSICAL”), will be created

automatically as the root of the class hierarchy, with all top level classes (classes with no

“Extends”) extending this class. You are free to define properties for this root class, by

including a class definition with Id=” ISM_ROOT_ PHYSICAL” in this sheet.

collection of Physical Classes in the target Class Library
model

Sheet: 'ISM Document Classes'

This sheet defines Document Classes. An abstract class, “Root Document

Class” (@id=“ISM_ROOT_DOCUMENT”), will be created automatically as the root of the

class hierarchy, with all top level classes (classes with no “Extends”) extending this class.

You are free to define properties for this root class, by including a class definition with

Id=” ISM_ROOT_DOCUMENT” in this sheet.

3.13

Sheet: 'ISM General Classes'

This sheet defines General Classes and Sub Classes. Classes that do not fall into one of the

categories Functional, Physical or Document. For example, System, Area and Contractor.

An abstract class, “Root General Class” (@id=“ISM_ROOT_GENERAL”), will be created

automatically as the root of the class hierarchy, with all top level classes (classes with no

“Extends”) extending this class. You are free to define properties for this root class, by

including a class definition with Id=” ISM_ROOT_ GENERAL” in this sheet.

Item Column Name

Constraint Description

1

Id

Required

2

3

4

5

Name

Name
{language}

Description

Description
{language}

The identifier for the Class definition. There is a
requirement that this value is unique within the
collection of General Classes in the target Class Library
model.

· Even though it is not an absolute requirement, it is
recommended that identifier values do not contain
white space characters.
To avoid identifier naming conflicts in applications
implementing the Class Library it is recommended that
all identifiers, across all concepts, are unique within a
Class Library. This can easily be achieved by using a
naming convention, such as “TAG_nnnn”.
It is strongly recommended, when a Class Library
evolves over time, to never change the value of any
identifiers.

· A human readable name for the Class definition.
· A human readable name for the Class definition, in a

specific language.
The value for {language} is composed as
{languageCode}-{countryCode} (see the start of this
section).
This column can be repeated as many times as
required, each with a different value for {language}
· An arbitrary description for the purpose/role of the

Class.

· An arbitrary description of the purpose/role of the

Class, in a specific language.
The value for {language} is composed as
{languageCode}-{countryCode} (see the start of this
section).

The value of this column should contain the identifier
of a Naming Template definition to associate with the

If you need to associate more than one Naming
Template to a Class, you have to use the Document
Class Naming Templates sheet.

Specifies the applicability of the Naming Template. For
example, a Naming Template containing pattern
matching regular expressions will only be applicable
for Validation.
It is possible to specify more than one value, in which
case the values need to be separated by a single space
character.

11

Aspect

is applicable for the Class.

The identifier of the General Class being parent of
target Class. For example, when defining a General
Sub Class, this column typically contains the identifier
of the parent General Class.

· When extending a parent class, all aspects (properties,

attributes, naming templates, extensions) of the
parent class will automatically be derived by the target
class. Any explicit definitions on the target class will
override corresponding values being derived from
parent class.

· Annotating a Class with an aspect is a way to associate

the Class with a specific semantic definition.
Application logic will look for these aspects, and apply
associated processing rules. Definition of available
aspect values is outside the scope of this document.

whether, obsoleting is implemented might vary across

the AVEVA ISM user interface.

· Classes not having a value specified for this property
will be ordered as if they have the highest SortOrder
value.

3.14

Sheet: 'ISM General Class Attributes'

This sheet defines association of Permissible Attributes (or Characteristics) with General

Classes.

If you have Permissible Attributes common to all General Classes, you can specify them as

permissible on a class with Id=”ISM_ROOT_ GENERAL” (see Sheet: “ISM General Classes”),

and they will automatically be derived as permissible on all General Classes.

Sheet: 'ISM UoM Units'

This sheet defines Unit Of Measure Units.

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

1

Id

Required

2

3

Name

Name
{language}

The identifier for the Unit definition. There is a
requirement that this value is unique within the
collection of Units in the target Class Library model
· Even though it is not an absolute requirement, it is
recommended that identifier values do not contain
white space characters
To avoid identifier naming conflicts in applications
implementing the Class Library it is recommended
that all identifiers, across all concepts, are unique
within a Class Library. This can easily be achieved by
using a naming convention, such as “UNIT_nnnn”.
It is strongly recommended, when a Class Library
evolves over time, to never change the value of any
identifiers.

· A human readable name for the Unit definition, like

“kilograms” and “centimetre squared”.

· A human readable name for the Unit definition, in a

specific language.
The value for {language} is composed as
{languageCode}-{countryCode} (see the start of this
section)

The symbol associated with the Unit, like “Kg”, “MB”
and “cm2”.  This is the value that typically will suffix
the associated Attribute value in a user interface.
If missing the value of Name will be used.
· A value referencing the industry standard that

defines the Unit.

· A value specifying what measure system the Unit

belongs to.

The name of the group that the Unit belongs to. The
value will be used to group the collection of Units in
the AVEVA ISM user interface.

· Groups are created automatically, based on the

values provided in this column.

· Annotating a Unit with an aspect is a way to associate

the Unit with a specific semantic definition.
Application logic will look for these aspects, and
apply associated processing rules. Definition of
available aspect values is outside the scope of this
document.

· When evolving a Class Library over time, removing

previously existing definitions might cause issues with
consuming software systems (for example, they
might not support deleting a Unit that is in use for
existing data). Obsoleting deprecated definitions
might therefore be a less intrusive approach. How, or
whether, obsoleting is supported might vary across
consuming software though.

· Decides on the order in which Units are presented in

the AVEVA ISM user interface.

· Units not having a value specified for this property

will be ordered as if they have the highest SortOrder
value.

3.19

Sheet: 'ISM Enumerations'

This sheet defines Enumeration Lists with Items (also called List-Of-Values), typically used

for constraining valid values for Attributes.

Release 2.2

Page 36

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

·

AVEVA Information Standards Manager Importing from MS Excel Guide

CHAPTER 3 - Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA ISM Intrinsic Definitions

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

1

Id

Required

2

3

4

5

Name

Name
{language}

Description

Description
{language}

· An Enumeration is a List with Items, and potentially
sub-Items. The rows in this sheet therefore either
represent a List, or they represent an Item belonging
to a list.
If the value provided do not contain a “|” (pipe), the
entire value is considered a List identifier. There is a
requirement that List identifiers are unique within
the collection of Enumeration Lists in the target Class
Library model

· Even though it is not an absolute requirement, it is
recommended that List identifier values do not
contain white space characters
It is strongly recommended, when a Class Library
evolves over time, to never change the value of any
identifiers
If the value provided consists of multiple segments
delimited by “|” (pipe), the first segment is
considered the List identifier, while the successive
segments is considered Item identifiers, optionally
sub-Item identifiers.

· An Enumeration List can typically be used as a

ValidationRule for an Attribute; to constrain what
values will be accepted for the corresponding
attribute. An information authoring application
consuming the Class Library might choose to render
the List as a drop-down list in the user interface.
When a user makes a selection in this list, it is
typically the identifier value for the corresponding
Item that is considered the data value. Therefore the
notation, and the rules, for Item identifiers are
different from other identifier values in the Class
Library. So instead of using a notation like
“ITEM_nnn”, you should specify the data value that
the item represents. For example, a List that
represents possible “Cooling Types” might have Items
with identifiers like “AIR”, “WATER”.

· A human readable name for the List or Item

definition

· A human readable name for the List or Item

definition, in a specific language
The value for {language} is composed as
{languageCode}-{countryCode} (see the start of this
section)
This column can be repeated as many times as
required, each with a different value for {language}
· An arbitrary description of the purpose/role of the

List or Item

· An arbitrary description of the purpose/role of the

List or Item, in a specific language

3.20

Sheet: 'ISM N&N Elements'

This sheet defines Naming & Numbering Elements, to be used when composing Naming

Templates.

As an example, a Tag name is typically a composition of multiple values. The rule for each

of these values is defined by an Element. A Naming Template is then defined as a

collection of Elements, which in sum defines all the aspects of the Tag name. An example

from the NORSOK Coding System describes the Naming Template for lines consisting of

the following Elements:

Release 2.2

Page 38

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

·
·
·
AVEVA Information Standards Manager Importing from MS Excel Guide

CHAPTER 3 - Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA ISM Intrinsic Definitions

The interpretation of the rules described for an Element might vary across different

implementing software applications. The description below is based on how the AVEVA
Engineering Numbering Clock is implementing the rules.

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

1

Id

Required

2

3

4

5

Name

Name
{language}

Description

Description
{language}

6

Source

The identifier for the Element definition. There is a
requirement that this value is unique within the
collection of Elements in the target Class Library
model.

· Even though it is not an absolute requirement, it is
recommended that identifier values do not contain
white space characters.
To avoid identifier naming conflicts in applications
implementing the Class Library it is recommended that
all identifiers, across all concepts, are unique within a
Class Library. This can easily be achieved by using a
naming convention, such as “ELEM_xxxx”, where
“xxxx” indicates the role/purpose of the Element.
It is strongly recommended, when a Class Library
evolves over time, to never change the value of any
identifiers.

· A human readable name for the Element definition.
· A human readable name for the Element definition, in

a specific language.
The value for {language} is composed as
{languageCode}-{countryCode} (see the start of this
section).
This column can be repeated as many times as
required, each with a different value for {language}
· An arbitrary description of the purpose/role of the

Element.

· An arbitrary description of the purpose/role of the

Element, in a specific language.
The value for {language} is composed as
{languageCode}-{countryCode} (see the start of this
section).
This column can be repeated as many times as
required, each with a different value for {language}
· When composing an object name (for example, a Tag
name), the value for each of the Elements constituting
the name is typically picked from an Attribute of the
object. Source specifies either the Id or the Aspect of
that source Attribute.
If the value is enclosed in curly braces, Source is
referring to the Aspect of the source Attribute.
If the value is not enclosed in curly braces, the
Attribute is located by Id, or Aspect, in that order of
priority.

· When composing an object name, some of the

Elements might be considered optional. For example, if
the corresponding Attribute do not contain a value.
This indicator specifies whether the Element is
mandatory.

This value dictates how the Element should appear in
the composed name when the corresponding Attribute
value is empty.
If true, the Element, including any Prefix or Suffix will
be excluded from the composed name.

This value dictates the maximum number of characters
in the value represented by the Element.

This value dictates the minimum number of characters
in the value represented by the Element.
If the value provided contains less than this number of
characters, the value will be padded by
PaddingCharacter. The padding will be left or right,
depending on the data type of the associated Attribute
(see Source).

Specifies the character to use when padding the
Element value (see MinNumberOfCharacters).

Specifies a character sequence to prefix the Element
value when composing an object name.
Typically either Prefix or Suffix is used for an Element,
but rarely both on the same Element.

Specifies a character sequence to suffix the Element
value when composing an object name.
Typically either Prefix or Suffix is used for an Element,
but rarely both on the same Element.

Specifies a value to use as a replacement when the
associated Attribute (see Source) is empty.

Specifies a fixed value to use for the Element.
If a value is specified for this property, Source should
be empty.

Specifies a regular expression to be used when
validating the Element in an object name.
The expression specified will be used in composing a
holistic regular expression for the entire object name,
and should therefore not contain any positional
anchoring instructions.
If a value is specified for this property, Source should
be empty.
If a Naming Template contains one or more Elements
having a regular expression, the template will be
applicable for validation of exiting object names only.
Such a type of templates will not be applicable for
allocating new object names.

· An object name typically contains at least one Element

representing a running sequence number.
To avoid sequence number conflicts across object
types. For example, for Tags and Documents it is
recommended to use a distinct SequenceId for each
target object type.
This value specifies the sequence id to use when
providing sequence numbers for new object names.

· Annotating an Element with an aspect is a way to
associate the Element with a specific semantic
definition. Application logic will look for these aspects,
and apply associated processing rules. Definition of
available aspect values is outside the scope of this
document.

· When evolving a Class Library over time, removing

previously existing definitions might cause issues with
consuming software systems (for example, they might
not support deleting an Element that is in use for
existing data). Obsoleting deprecated definitions might
therefore be a less intrusive approach. How, or
whether, obsoleting is implemented might vary across
consuming software though.

· Decides on the order in which Elements are presented

in the AVEVA ISM user interface.

· Elements not having a value specified for this property
will be ordered as if they have the highest SortOrder
value.

3.21

Sheet: 'ISM N&N Templates'

This sheet defines Naming & Numbering Naming Templates.

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

1

Id

Required

The identifier for the Naming Template definition.
There is a requirement that this value is unique
within the collection of Naming Templates in the
target Class Library model.

· Even though it is not an absolute requirement, it is
recommended that identifier values do not contain
white space characters.
To avoid identifier naming conflicts in applications
implementing the Class Library it is recommended
that all identifiers, across all concepts, are unique
within a Class Library. This can easily be achieved by
using a naming convention, such as “TPL_xxxx” ,
where “xxxx” indicates the role/purpose of the
Naming Template.

Specifies the applicability of the Naming Template.
If naming rules are introduced, for example, a Brown
Field asset, it is very likely that there are existing
object names not conforming to the naming standard
being introduced. It is typically not practical to
rename these existing objects. Naming templates
applicable for Validation only can then be used to
validate the names of existing objects, while naming
templates applicable for Allocation can be used when
allocating names to new objects.
It is possible to specify more than one value, in which
case the values need to be separated by a single
space character.

Specifies a regular expression reflecting the entire
name structure represented by the Naming
Template.
If specified, the Naming template should not have
any Permissible Elements.

· A Naming Template having a regular expression will

be applicable for Validation only.

· Annotating a Naming Template with an aspect is a

way to associate the Naming Template with a specific
semantic definition. Application logic will look for
these aspects, and apply associated processing rules.
Definition of available aspect values is outside the
scope of this document.

SortOrder has higher significance for Permissible
Elements than for other concepts in the Class Library,
as it dictates in what order the Elements will appear in
the composed object name. Therefore, if not specified,
a value will be provided automatically, reflecting the
order in which the Permissible Elements appear in the
spread sheet.

3.23

Sheet: 'ISM Maturity Levels'

This sheet contains definitions of Maturity Levels.

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

1

{language}

Presence

- Not

Applicable

- Optional

- Required

The identifier for the Maturity Level definition. There is
a requirement that this value is unique within the
collection of Maturity Levels in the target Class Library
model.
If zero, or a positive integer value, is specified, this
value will represent the magnitude of the maturity
level.
If a non-zero, non-positive integer value is specified,
SortOrder will have to be supplied with the value
representing the magnitude of the maturity level.
The value ‘{null}’ can be used for a maturity level
definition representing “no maturity level selected” in
the AVEVA ISM user interface.

· A human readable name for the Maturity Level

definition.

· A human readable name for the Maturity Level

definition, in a specific language.
The value for {language} is composed as
{languageCode}-{countryCode} (see the start of this
section).
This column can be repeated as many times as
required, each with a different value for {language}
· An arbitrary description of the purpose/role of the

Maturity Level.

· An arbitrary description of the purpose/role of the

Maturity Level, in a specific language.
The value for {language} is composed as
{languageCode}-{countryCode} (see the start of this
section).
This column can be repeated as many times as
required, each with a different value for {language}
· When a Maturity Level is associated with an Attribute,
or a Permissible Attribute, the Presence value of the
Maturity Level takes precedence over the default
Presence value of the attribute, depending on the
maturity of a data object.

· As an example, assume an Attribute has 0, 5, and 8 as
Permissible Maturity Levels. When a data object is at
maturity 0-4, the Presence value defined for Maturity
Level 0 will take precedence, when the data object is at
maturity 5-7, the Presence value defined for Maturity
Level 5 will take precedence, the Maturity Level  with
the closest match to the objects maturity will be
considered the “ruling” Maturity Level for the object.

· Annotating a Maturity Level with an aspect is a way to
associate the Maturity Level with a specific semantic
definition. Application logic will look for these aspects,
and apply associated processing rules. Definition of
available aspect values is outside the scope of this
document.

· When evolving a Class Library over time, removing

previously existing definitions might cause issues with
consuming software systems (for example, they might
not support deleting a Maturity Level that is in use for
existing data). Obsoleting deprecated definitions might
therefore be a less intrusive approach. How, or
whether, obsoleting is implemented might vary across
consuming software though.

· Decides on the order in which Maturity Levels are

presented in the AVEVA ISM user interface.
If the Id property is a non-zero, non-positive integer
value, the value for SortOrder will represent the
magnitude of the maturity level, and should have the
value of zero or higher.

· Consuming software systems might also utilise this

information to decide on in which order Maturity Level
values are to be presented.

3.24

Sheet: 'ISM Life Cycle Types'

This sheet contains sequences of Maturity Levels representing a life cycle.

The main purpose of Life Cycle Types is to support the assumption that different data

objects (of different classes) might have different number of maturity gates (applicable

Maturity Levels) in their life cycle. By associating a Life Cycle Type with a Functional Class or

a Document Class, one can predict what maturity gates objects of those classes will have to

pass in their life cycle.

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

1

Id

Required
- A positive
integer
value

The identifier for the Life Cycle Type definition. There is
a requirement that this value is unique within the
collection of Life Cycle Types in the target Class Library
model.

· Even though it is not an absolute requirement, it is
recommended that identifier values do not contain
white space characters.

document.

therefore be a less intrusive approach. How, or

Item Column Name Constraint

Description
{language}

Taxonomy is a hierarchical structure consisting of the
Taxonomy itself, with a collection of Nodes, which
themselves can have collection of Nodes, and so on.
The rows in this sheet therefore either represent a
Taxonomy, or they represent a Node.
If the value provided do not contain a “|” (pipe), the
entire value is considered a Taxonomy identifier.
There is a requirement that Taxonomy identifiers are
unique within the collection of Taxonomies in the
target Class Library model

· Even though it is not an absolute requirement, it is
recommended that List identifier values do not
contain white space characters
It is strongly recommended, when a Class Library
evolves over time, to never change the value of any
identifiers
If the value provided consists of multiple segments
delimited by “|” (pipe), the first segment is
considered the Taxonomy identifier, while the
successive segments is considered Node identifiers, in
a hierarchy as deep as you like.

· A human readable name for the Taxonomy or Node

definition

definition, in a specific language

required, each with a different value for {language}

Taxonomy or Node

Taxonomy or Node, in a specific language

CHAPTER 3 - Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA ISM Intrinsic Definitions

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

3

Property
Name

Required

4

Property
Value

The name of an extension property or node. There
are three alternatives syntaxes:
[namespacePrefix:][node1/…/nodeN/]
propertyName
Specifies a single property, optionally with a list of
ancestor nodes. If no namespacePrefix is specified,
the DefaultNamespacePrefix will be used.
If the expression is suffixed with an exclamation mark
(“propertyName!”), the property will be qualified as
not derivable.
[namespacePrefix:]node1[/…/nodeN]/_group
Specifies a node for a group of properties, optionally
with a list of ancestor nodes. If no namespacePrefix is
specified, the DefaultNamespacePrefix will be used.
Consecutive rows containing “/
propertyName” (notice the initial slash character) will
be added as properties for the group node. The
group ends with the first row not containing a
property name prefixed with slash.

If the expression is suffixed with an exclamation mark
(“…/_group!”), all properties in the group will be
qualified as not derivable.
[namespacePrefix:]node1[/…/nodeN]/_table

Specifies a node for a property table, optionally with
a list of ancestor nodes. If no namespacePrefix is
specified, the DefaultNamespacePrefix will be used.

Consecutive columns in the same row represents
headers in the property table, and therefore also the
property names.
Consecutive rows containing “/nodeName” (notice
the initial slash character) will be added as a sub-
node, representing a row in the property table.
Consecutive columns in the same row represents
property values for the property specified in the
corresponding column in the first table row. The
table ends with the first row not containing a node
name prefixed with slash.

If the expression is suffixed with an exclamation mark
(“…/_table!”), all properties in the table will be
qualified as not derivable.

· Represents the value of the corresponding property

For examples of Extensions Sheet, see the ISM Standard Excel Format v1.1.0.xlsx example

Excel Workbook.

CHAPTER 4 - Annotation of Intrinsic Definition for AVEVA NET Workhub Consumption

4

Annotation of Intrinsic Definition for AVEVA NET
Workhub Consumption

AVEVA NET Workhub™ extensions are defined in the namespace:

· Prefix: anet
· Uri: http://schemas.aveva.com/Generic/Engineering.Information/

Class.Library/Model/AVEVA_NET/2012/11

In addition to the dedicated sheets targeting AVEVA NET Workhub configurations, it is also

possible to annotate some of the intrinsic definitions with aspects for configuring AVEVA

NET Workhub.

To further control how AVEVA NET Workhub is configured, you can also extend intrinsic

definitions with an arbitrary number of “expando” columns:

· An expando column header has to be prefixed by the AVEVA NET Workhub

extension namespace prefix.

· The expando column header (without the prefix) will be added as a property to

the target construct in the AVEVA NET Workhub Class Library.

· As an example, if a column header “anet:Scale” is added to the sheet “ISM UoM
Class Units”, the value in this column will result in <Scale>{column value}</

Scale> being added to the permissible unit for the target Measure Class

definition in the AVEVA NET Workhub Class Library.

Release 2.2

Page 52

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA Information Standards Manager Importing from MS Excel Guide

CHAPTER 4 - Annotation of Intrinsic Definition for AVEVA NET Workhub Consumption

4.1

Suppress entities from being consumed by AVEVA NET Workhub

By annotating an entity with the extension property anet:suppress=”true”, the entity will

be suppressed from the AVEVA NET Workhub configuration. This property is applicable for

entities in the following sheets:

ISM Attributes

ISM Functional Classes

ISM Functional Class Attributes

ISM Document Classes

ISM Document Class Attributes

ISM UoM Units

ISM UoM Classes

ISM UoM Class Units

For example, if you do not want the automatically generated classes

ISM_ROOT_FUNCTIONAL (see Sheet: “ISM Functional Classes”) and

ISM_ROOT_DOCUMENT (see Sheet: “ISM Document Classes”) to be included in the

AVEVA NET Workhub class hierarchies, you can add these class definitions to the

respective sheets manually, and then annotate them with anet:suppress=”true”. To

prevent this annotation from being derived by child classes, remember to suffix the

column name with an exclamation mark, for example, anet:suppress! (see Sheet: “ISM

Extension Namespaces”). The result will be that these classes are suppressed from the

AVEVA NET Workhub configuration, but their child classes will still be included. The child

classes will then by default get the parent class id of the suppressed parent as their

parent class id.

aspects

4.5

How to associate attributes with ANWH data sets

By default each non-abstract Functional Class will have an associated ANWH data set

defined, containing all the class permissible attributes as either Characteristic or Property

definitions. This data set is known as the default data set for the class. The class identifier

for the default data set is composed as specified by the DataSetClassId property in Sheet:

“ISM ANWH GEICL Mappings”.

In addition to the default data set, you can define any number of auxiliary data sets, by

use of extension properties in Sheet: “ISM Attributes”:

Item Extension property EIWM Property Default Value

1

anet:Dataset
/Specific/{xxx}

- true
- false

2

anet:Dataset
/Shared/{xxx}

- true
- false

· When true, an auxiliary data set will be defined
for each Functional Class having the associated
attribute as permissible
You replace the placeholder {xxx} with a name of
your choice
The class identifier for this data set will be
composed as specified by the DataSetClassId
{xxx} property in Sheet: “ISM ANWH GEICL
Mappings”

· One auxiliary data set will be defined, to contain
all attributes that fulfils the following conditions:
o Have true is this column
o Is permissible for a single Root Functional

Class, as explained in Sheet: “ISM Functional
Classes”

Item Extension property EIWM Property Default Value

3

anet:Dataset
/Default

- true
- false

The class identifier for this data set will be
composed as specified by the DataSetClassId
{xxx} property in Sheet: “ISM ANWH GEICL
Mappings”, getting {classId} from the property
ParentClassId in Sheet: “ISM ANWH GEICL
Mappings”

· When false, the associated attribute will be
excluded from the default data set for all
Functional Classes
In cases where a data set ends up having no
associated attributes, the data set class will be
suppressed from the ANWH definition

4.6

Annotate 'Measure Classes Collection' with AVEVA NET
Workhub-specific Aspects

The following extension columns are supported for this sheet:

Item Extension property EIWM Property Default Value

1

anet:PrefixClassIds

- true
- false

To be declared on ‘Measure Classes Collection'.
· By setting it to ‘false ‘, prefix ‘MC_’ will not be

added to ‘class id’.

· Default value is ‘true’ and if this extension

property is not specified, application will treat it
as ‘true’.

2

anet:SuppressGeneral - true
- false

To be declared on ‘Measure Classes Collection'.

· By setting it to ‘true ‘, general class will be

suppressed.

· Default value is ‘false’ and if this extension

property is not specified, application will treat it
as ‘false’.

4.7

Annotate 'Measure Units Collection' with AVEVA NET Workhub
specific Aspects

The following extension columns are supported for this sheet:

Item Extension property EIWM Property Default Value

1

anet:ClassId

To be declared on ‘Measure Units’ collection.
This extension property is to override default
value of class id on ‘System UOM
Class’ (Currently its defaulting to SI UOM
System).

2

anet:ClassName

To be declared on ‘Measure Units’ collection.

CHAPTER 5 - Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA NET Workhub Extensions

5

Sheets and Column Headers for AVEVA NET Workhub
Extensions

These sheets contains configuration that will be utilised by the AVEVA NET Workhub

Configurator when generating Class Library configuration targeting AVEVA NET Workhub.

In addition to the columns explicitly described, each sheet can also contain an arbitrary

number of “expando” columns:

· An expando column header has to be prefixed by the AVEVA NET Workhub

extension namespace prefix.

· The expando column header (without the prefix) will be added as a property to

the target construct in the AVEVA NET Workhub Class Library.

· As an example, if a column header “anet:NonSearchable” is added to the sheet

“ISM ANWH Classes”, the value in this column will result in

<NonSearchable>{column value}</NonSearchable> being added to the class

definition in the AVEVA NET Workhub Class Library.

5.1

Sheet: 'ISM ANWH AssociationTypes'

This sheet contains definitions of AVEVA NET Workhub specific Association Types, and is

therefore applicable only when the resulting Class Library is supposed to be consumed by

an AVEVA NET Workhub instance.

In the resulting Class Library model this information will be held as AVEVA NET Workhub

specific extensions to the Class Library root node.

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

1

2

3

4

Id

Required

Name

Required

SourceRole

Required

TargetRole

Required

Specifies the identifier property for the AVEVA NET
Workhub Association Type

Specifies name property for the AVEVA NET
Workhub Association Type

Specifies Source Role property for the AVEVA NET
Workhub Association Type

Specifies Target Role property for the AVEVA NET
Workhub Association Type

5.4

Sheet: 'ISM ANWH ObjectContexts'

This sheet contains definitions of how object contexts will be composed in an AVEVA NET

Workhub instance, and is therefore applicable only when the resulting Class Library is

supposed to be consumed by an AVEVA NET Workhub instance.

In the resulting Class Library model this information will be held as AVEVA NET Workhub

specific extensions to the Class Library root node.

Item Column Name Constraint

Description

1

AssetId

Asset
Description

ClassId

Required

Object

Specifies the instance value for an asset identifier in

Specifies the human readable value for the asset
name

Specifies a verbose description for the asset

Specifies an AVEVA NET Workhub specific class
identifier for the object context
If ObjectContext has a value, this column also has to
contain a value

Specifies the context value for instances if ClassId in
the target AVEVA NET Workhub instance
If ClassId has a value, this column also has to contain
a value

This sheet can be extended with columns holding arbitrary extensions, as described in

Sheet: “ISM Extension Namespaces”. Extensions can be added to Assets only, not to

individual Class/ObjectContext. To add extensions to an Asset, create a row where you

leave ClassId and ObjectContext empty, and provide values for the extension columns.

5.5

Sheet: 'ISM ANWH PermissibleAssoc'

This sheet contains definitions of Permissible Associations between classes in the Class

Library model. These definitions are primarily intended to be included in an AVEVA NET

Workhub specific composition of the Class Library, but they are also utilized by AVEVA NET

Gatekeeper and AVEVA Change Manager in communication with an AVEVA NET Workhub

instance.

using the identifier specified in this column to apply
values for permissible associations to the object in
the AVEVA NET Workhub instance model

AVEVA Information Standards Manager User Guide

CHAPTER 1 - Preface

1

Preface

This chapter gives basic information about this guide, training and support.

1.1

About this Guide

The AVEVA Information Standards Manager 2.2.1 User Interface (UI) is built using a

consistent interface, for example, input fields, grids or hierarchical structures.

Understanding these interface elements is key to using the UI efficiently.

This guide describes how to:

Log on to AVEVA Information Standards Manager

· Understand and use the interface elements

Interpret colours and icons
· Create or import Standards
· Create information association and interconnections

1.2

Audience

This guide is intended for anyone who uses AVEVA Information Standards Manager. It is

assumed that you are familiar with using the Internet and more specifically, the

organisation of engineering information, processes and standards used in your company.

1.3

Summary of Sections

Section Name

Description

Product Overview

Overview of AVEVA Information Standards Manager.

Getting Started

Explains the prerequisites for using AVEVA Information Standards
Manager. Shows how to log on, access the Homepage, create
User Roles, colour coding, catalogue icons and actions, grids and
tokens. Also, provides a high-level understanding of the user
interface. For example, importing standards, catalogue actions
and icons, managing users and viewing Standard details.

Managing Users

Provides details on managing users, assigning permissions and
maintaining user groups.

Managing the
Catalogue

Using Classes

Describes Creating folders, working on Standards, viewing
Standard details and tagging a release.

Describes Classes and provides details about permissible
attributes and network.

CHAPTER 1 - Preface

1.5

Product and Training Information

For information about product training courses, see the Product Training section of

AVEVA's website (http://www.aveva.com/training), or contact the nearest AVEVA

Regional Support Centre (http://support.aveva.com/support).

1.6

Technical Support

If you encounter software issues or have suggestions for software improvements, contact

the AVEVA Helpdesk (https://avevahelpdesk.aveva.com), and register a support incident.

After you register a support incident, we know your identity (from your customer ID) and

the details of the incident. We endeavour to fix the software issue or consider the

software improvement suggestion as soon as possible, and notify you about the status of

the incident.

If you are an accredited user of the AVEVA products, you are entitled to use the AVEVA

Helpdesk, which you should have valid login credentials. For any other queries, contact

the nearest AVEVA Regional Support Centre (http://support.aveva.com/support).

Release 2.2.1

AVEVA Information Standards Manager User Guide

CHAPTER 2 - Product Overview

2

Product Overview

AVEVA Information Standards Manager is part of AVEVA’s Information Engineering suite

of products within the AVEVA Enterprise portfolio. It is a multi-layered, Standards-driven

conceptual model that encompasses multiple overlapping Standards and Class Libraries,

which qualitatively improves your engineering information. It provides the essential

means for robust and visible management of the continual changes to information in the

digital asset. It also provides information about changes, large and small, made to the

physical asset during its lifecycle.

Release 2.2.1

3

Getting Started

This chapter introduces you to AVEVA Information Standards Manager. Specifically, this

chapter provides information on the following topics:

Logging on to AVEVA Information Standards Manager

Prerequisites

Accessing the Home Page

Understanding menus and basic page components (User Interface elements)

Key concepts

User roles

3.1

Prerequisites

Assumptions

This document assumes that AVEVA Information Standards Manager is installed and

deployed using the instructions provided in AVEVA Information Standards Manager

Installation Guide, and that you use AVEVA Information Standards Manager in accordance

with the requirements, procedures and policies of your company.

Prerequisites

For using AVEVA Information Standards Manager, ensure that your account is created by

the System Administrator and you are granted the required permissions.

CHAPTER 3 - Getting Started

3.2

Understanding the User Interface

The user interface consists of the following:

· The left side of the blue bar is used for Catalogues. You can use these Catalogues
to manage Users and Standards. Whilst in the Catalogue view, you can select,

import or create Standards. When no Standards are selected you get a tree view

of folders that contain Compositions and Standards. If you select a Standard, the

default view is of the active Standard and its audit trail. For understanding

Catalogues, see Managing the Catalogue. For understanding how to manage

users, see Managing Users and User Groups.

· The blue bar is for Content Discovery (for details, see Content Discover Sidebar).
The right side of the blue bar is for content. The content area is a tabbed panel

where each tab displays details for a given entity in the Standard. The Browse

section provides accessibility to the underlying functions, such as Classes,

Attributes, Units of Measure and Taxonomy, depending on what you select from

the Content Discovery Sidebar.

· The browsing area is for navigation and use. The details on icons and icon

annotations. Use this area for creating and managing Class Libraries, Attributes,

Units of Measure, searching, and maintaining lists of favourite items.

· A global toolbar is displayed at the top of the content area. It is reserved for the

following information:

· Default Favourite List: Provides the default list of objects marked as

favourites. To create a list of favourites, or edit an existing list, see Using

Favourites Lists.

Release 2.2.1

· Standards Details: Provides the details of the Standard currently locked by

you. Alternatively, you may click the Details button (

) on Catalogue. See

Viewing and Configuring Standards Details.

Logged-in User: Displays the current user's name.

Information and Help (

): Provides the summary of the user guide, such

as key concepts, UI, audit trail, icon, icon annotations and grid.

· The Content area is where you will manage your data.

3.3

Key Concepts

Descriptions for common words used in the AVEVA Information Standards Manager

vocabulary:

Word

Abstract

Description

An Abstract class is the base definition for more specialised classes. For
example, Instrument Equipment is an abstract and a parent class for
Alarm Panel. In an authoring or managing tool, they would have
instantiated objects.

Release 2.2.1

Page 13

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

·
·
AVEVA Information Standards Manager User Guide

CHAPTER 3 - Getting Started

Word

Aspect

Description

A semantic categorisation that can be used to create rules that help
process and populate similar or linked values.

Class Concept

Identifies the purpose of a Class type:
· Functional Class Concept identifies that the Class relates to the design

work.

· Physical Class Concept identifies that the Class relates to the Assets.
· Document Class Concept identifies that the Class relates to

Documents.

· General Class Concept identifies that the Class relates to everything

else.

Class Library

A collection of Standards constituting a holistic definition of Classes,
Attributes and related information.

Composition

A collection of independent Standards that make up a Class Library.

Concept

Identifies whether an object is permissible on a specific Class Concept.
· An attribute can relate to, or may be permissible on, a Functional

Class as well as a Physical Class.

Derived

Discipline

Entity

Extends/
Extension

Hierarchy

Language

The entity or value is inherited from a parent in the entity hierarchy.

A specialised team, which has the responsibility for the identified item.
Different teams may have responsibility for the item over its life cycle.

Any item in the Standard that can have an identifier designated. For
example, Functional Classes, Physical Classes, Document Classes,
Attributes, Enumerated Lists and UoM Unit.

Additional user-defined metadata on an entity.

A structured order of information and properties in a system where the
allocated properties are inherited by its child items.

Help us to define and identify the same item in different languages used
on a Project or Asset. For example, English and Norwegian.

Namespace

A shareable class of unique elements. For example, a set of symbols or
file types.

Nodes

A point within a structured order of information.

Nomenclature AVEVA Information Standards Manager uses standard terminology.
However, the use of terminology could differ based on standards. It
helps map the terminologies use across different standards. For
example, CFIHOS and ISO.

Permissible

An entity being associated with another entity.

Standard

A defined procedure or level of work mandated by project company or
standards body.

CHAPTER 3 - Getting Started

3.4

User Roles

To manage individual users access to functions, features and tasks in the application, the

user privileges are organised by user Roles. A Role is a set of permissions that are granted

to a user. It controls the access to application modules and the work flow. A user can

have only one active Role, which can only be administered and managed by the Tool

Administrator. Each Role determines the authorisation granted to a user to perform

specific operations. All users that have a specific Role assigned inherit the privileges for

that Role.

A Role with higher capacity will include the privileges for all other lesser capacity Roles.

For example, if a user has the Administrator Role, they enjoy all privileges for the Creator

and the Administrator Roles. Any user permissions granted on an individual folder or

Standard will be restricted by the role the user currently has. This means that a Read-only

user will not be able to be assign any edit permissions. The Role cannot be amended

through the UI. For more information, refer to AVEVA Information Standards Manager

Installation Guide.

The following table provides a summary of Roles in AVEVA Information Standards

Manager:

Role Name

Responsibility

Administrator Only a user with Administrator Role can unlock a Standard that may be

locked by another user for editing or permanently delete an Obsolete
Standard. This enables an additional layer of security. Before the
Administrator unlocks the Standard, they must first save it and tag it
appropriately before making further changes.

Creator

Author

Reader

Creates and edits Standards and the Catalogue folders. Can make a
Standard Obsolete. Cannot permanently delete a Standard.

Edits Standards, sets Class hierarchies. Cannot create a Standard.

Does not have edit permissions. Can only view the information.

3.5

Colour Coding

Colour coding is used on input fields and tokens to display information on the

modification or derived state of the entity or value. The following lists the backgrounds

and their meanings:

Colour

Explanation

The entity or value has been derived from elsewhere. For example, an
inherited permissible attribute.

The entity or value have been modified.

The derived entity or value have been modified in an ancestor.

The entity or value have been added.

The entity or value have been deleted.

3.6

Input Field Borders

Borders on input fields are used to convey information on whether the contained value is

editable.

Border

Description

A solid border means that the value is editable.

A dashed border means that the value is read-only.

3.7

Grids

Grids display tabular data, for example, a list of permissible attributes for a class. The

following is an example of a grid:

Release 2.2.1

Page 16

© Copyright 2003 to current year. AVEVA Solutions Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

AVEVA Information Standards Manager User Guide

CHAPTER 3 - Getting Started

 All grids have some common features, such as:

· Sorting: By default, most grids are sorted by the sort order and name. Change

this sorting by clicking on the header. The first click sorts in the ascending order.

The second, in the descending order. The third click reverts the list to the default.

· Selecting Columns: Right-click a column header to choose a columns to display.
· Paging: Grids display limited entities per page. If the number of items in a grid

cannot fit a single page, the number of pages will appear at the bottom-center of

the grid. Use arrows keys to navigate between pages.

Item count: On the task bar of the grid, you will find information on the number

of items in the grid and what items are displayed on the current page.

· Adding items: Some grids support adding of items. In such grids, look for the Add

(

) button at the bottom left of the grid.

· Grouping: Some grids contain grouped data. Grouping is predefined in a grid and
it is not possible to group by an arbitrary field. Groups have precedence over

sorting and any sorting applied will sort items in the groups.

· Reorder: Drag columns in the desired order.

3.8

Tokens

A token is an annotated link to an entity. It represents the actual data that lies elsewhere

in the application. The following icon is an example of a token for the functional class

Agitator. Click the icon to access the details contained with the actual entity:

Tokens are annotated with colours. The background colour of the entity icon is

determined by the linked entity's modification state. For example, if the entity is modified

the icon appears yellow. The following is a token that has its entity modified, while its

name is derived from a parent:

For information on colours, see Colour Coding.

CHAPTER 4 - Managing Users

4

Managing Users

Click the User Groups icon (

) to access the list of users who can access AVEVA

Information Standards Manager. Users who have access to AVEVA Information Standards

Manager are listed under the Everyone user group. By default, the users have read-only

access to all those Standards and compositions placed in the Public folder in Catalogue.

Additionally, you can create a User Group to manage users rather than managing

everyone individually on the Catalogue folders and Standards.

4.4

Managing User Group Permissions

You can only assign and manage properties on groups. Right-click and select Properties to

view the default group settings.

To assign and manage properties for users:

1.

Select Everyone in the Permissions section. Otherwise, to view and assign

permissions for a user, select the required user from the section.

2.

In the Permissions section, select or clear the Allow or Deny check boxes that

correspond to the following capabilities to permit or restrict the user

accordingly: Read, Modify, Delete, Manage Permissions, Add Items and

Remove Items.

3.

Click Save.

AVEVA Information Standards Manager User Guide

CHAPTER 5 - Managing the Catalogue

5

Managing the Catalogue

The Catalogue area stages folders that contain Compositions and Standards. There are

four folders available out of the box. But, you can create a root folder, if required.

As part of the standard installation of AVEVA Information Standards Manager, the

following four folders are available:

· Frequently Used: Shortcuts for those Standards that you use frequently are

displayed here. To perform tasks on a Standard, right-click it within its parent

folder, and not within the Frequently Used folder.

· Undesignated: Standards that currently do not have any accessibility shortcuts

are placed in this folder and are visible to all users if they have required

permissions. For information, see Permissions for Descendants.

· Personal: Content is only visible to other users if they have permissions. You can
delete a Standard placed within this folder, unless you have assigned permissions

for other users to view and edit that Standard. If you have tagged a Standard in

the Personal folder, and permitted other users to access or use the Standard, you

cannot delete it even if you have the Creator Role.

· Public: Shared by all users. All Standards in the Public folder, irrespective of the

underlying access permissions, are visible to all users.

In addition to the default folders that are mentioned above, you can create a new folder

and drag the required Standard into it to assign access permissions.

To add a root folder:

1.

Right-click the empty area, and select Add Root Folder.

2.

Enter a name for the folder and click Add.

Creating a Standard

To create a Standard:

1.

2.

Right-click a root folder.

Select the Create new option. You can also click the Create (

) button on

Catalogue.

3.

In the Create new Standard window, enter the identifier, which is a

4.

Click Create.

A new Standard is created within the folder you right-clicked on. You can import an

existing Standard. For details, see Importing Standards.

5.1.3

Permissions for Descendants

To give the permissions for descendants, right-click a root folder or descendant folder and

or Select the Permissions for Descendants option. In the Permissions for Descendant

Folders and Shortcuts window, configure the permissions for the underlying sub-folders

and shortcuts.

Note: Assign permissions again to those objects that are created within the folder after

these permissions are set. Such objects will not inherit the permissions from their parent.

5.1.4

Root or Parent Folder Properties

Right-click the root folder or a parent folder to access the properties for that folder.

Everyone has Read-only permissions on those root folders available out of the box. But,

only the creator of a folder or the Administrator has permissions to edit the properties.

In the Details section, you can change the name of the folder. The Name is a mandatory

field. The Details section also provides the create and update histories and your currently

effective permissions.

Field Name

5.2

Working with Standards

The Catalogue enables you to import, select and create Standards. You are presented

with two views: Standards and Active Standard. The Standards view contains a list of

Standards, and the Active Standard view contains an audit trail.

· Standards list actions:

· Open: Opens the Standard
· Audit Trail: Shows the audit trail of the Standard without opening it

· Audit trail commit actions:

· Open: Opens the Change Set for viewing.
· Compare with Active Class Library: Compares the Change Set against Active

Standard/Change Set. Creates a temporary Standard.

· Merge into Active Class Library: Merges the content of the commit into the

Active Standard.

Note: You can access the Audit Trail for the latest 200 changes.

When you either create or import a Standard using the global toolbar, it appears in the

Personal folder. As long as you do not either tag a Standard in the folder or give other

users permissions to access or use to it, no other user can view or use the Standard. You

will be the only user (other than the Administrator) who has permissions to permanently

delete it in such a case.

5.2.1

Importing Standards

To import a Standard:

1.

Click the Import (

) button. You can also right-click a folder and select

2.

3.

4.

5.

6.

7.

Import from File.

Click Choose File and select the required document.

Choose from the options:

Import as a new Standard: This creates a new Standard in the Personal

folder.

· Merge into the Active Standard: Ensure that the Standard is locked for

editing before selecting this option.

· Merge into the copy of the active Standard

Click the Save As (

) button.

Create a new ID for your Standard and optionally enter a name and

description in the respective fields.

Note: If the Name field is left blank, the application auto-populates the ID in

that field.

Click Save As.

Click the Lock for Editing (

) button to lock the Standard to make changes.

Note: You must have the Creator Role to create or import a Standard. If you

have the Author Role, you can only edit an existing standard. For information,

see User Roles.

8.

After you have completed the changes, click the Validate (

) button to

accept the changes and to validate the Standard. For information, see

Validating Standards.

9.

Click either the Unlock and Save Changes (

) button to commit the changes

and unlock the Standard to make it available to everyone for use or click the

Unlock and Discard Changes (

) button to discard the changes. Optionally,

tag a release.

CHAPTER 5 - Managing the Catalogue

5.3

Viewing and Configuring Standard Details

Clicking the Details button (

) on Catalogue enables you to perform the following

actions:

Viewing Standards details

Viewing approval history

Creating or managing Compositions

Managing extensions

Managing validation rules

Implementing corresponding identifiers

5.3.1

Viewing Standards Details

To navigate through the metadata of the currently selected Standard:

1.

In the Details tab, view the required information:

a.

The unique identification for the Standard. This supports alphanumeric

characters, and does not auto-increment.

b.

The name in this optional field. If left blank, it shows the ID as the name.

This field accepts alphanumeric characters. This field is editable.

c.

The description of what the Standard contains. This field is editable.

2.

Select the Abstract check box if the currently selected Standard only contains

abstract Classes.

3.

The Version field is read-only and displays the current version number. It auto-

populates with the next four-digit version number when you check out the

Standard again.

4.

5.

The Version Date (UTC) registers the date of change. This field is read-only.

The Locked for editing by field displays the user who has currently locked the

Standard for editing.

6.

The Sort Order field displays the order in which the Standard is sorted within a

folder or composition.

5.3.2

Viewing Approval History

The fields on the Approved By tab display information after you perform Validation of the

currently Active Standard. The Name field populates the approver. The Version field

populates the approved version number. The Description field contains the details of the

validation. For information on validation, see Validating Standards.

CHAPTER 5 - Managing the Catalogue

5.3.3

Creating or Managing Compositions

Use the fields in this tab to add items to a Composition. If you have read-only privileges,

you cannot modify the information in this tab. However, you can reorder the records

displayed in a Composition. The Standards take precedence based on their order in the

composition.

To add a Composition:

1. On the Composition tab, click the Add button. From the dropdown in the Add

Items to the Composition window, select the required Catalogue Standard.

Note: Catalogue Standards previously added to the Standard do not display in

the dropdown.

2.

To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add.

Alternatively, click Add and Close.

3.

4.

The ID and Name fields on the Composition tab will display the added record.

The Required Version field displays the version required by the application.

You can use this field to set the version of the Standard to use. The version

histories of changes saved and tagged by you are listed in the dropdown for

this field. You can have this value set to a version which may be different from

the value in the Actual Version field, which is read-only.

5.

Click Close.

5.3.4

Managing Extensions

Extensions enable you to add metadata to an object. This can include configuration maps

that help configure the same value across multiple tools. Right-click the parent,

transitional folder to add extensions.

Release 2.2.1

CHAPTER 5 - Managing the Catalogue

Before you manage extensions ensure that you have the required top-level extensions

defined in the Extension Namespaces tab of Standard Details. Here you can define

properties with namespaces.

Defining Top-level Extension Namespaces

To define top-level extension namespaces:

1. On the Extension Namespaces tab, click the Add button to create an

extension namespace.

2.

In the Prefix field, enter a word of your choice. Ensure that the prefix name

does not contain a colon (:) or space, and does not start with a number.

3.

Enter a name for your extension namespace in the Name field.

4. Use the URI field to input a universal resource identifier (URI), which is a

universally unique string.

5. Optionally, in the Description field, provide a purposeful description of what

the extension namespace does. You may have applied the extension

namespace to Attributes, Standards, Enumerations or a Functional Class.

After defining top-level extension namespaces, you can define object-specific extensions.

The Extensions tab on Standards Details is reserved for managing extensions for other

applications, such as AVEVA NET Workhub and Dashboard. For information, refer to the

AVEVA Information Standards Manager Importing from MS Excel document.

Adding a Namespace

To add an object-level namespace:

1.

2.

Right-click a parent to add a namespace.

In the Add Extension Namespace Node window, select the required

namespace from the dropdown.

3.

To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add.

Alternatively, click Add and Close.

1.

To add an extension node, click Add Node.

2.

3.

To extend a property:

1.

Right-click Add Property to extend properties.

2.

Enter a unique property name, which is mandatory, along with its value in the

Select the Make Derivable check box, if required. In such a case, the new

object will derive properties from its parent.

4.

To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add.

Alternatively, click Add and Close.

CHAPTER 5 - Managing the Catalogue

5.4

Tagging a Release

To ensure that only the latest and updated Standard is used by others, you can tag a

released Standard. When you tag a saved Standard as Released, you increment the major

and minor release numbers. When you save a Standard, you increment the dotted

release numbers for a major release.

To tag a validated Standard as Released, use the following procedure:

1. Go to an Active Standard.

2.

Ensure that you do not have the Standard locked for editing. This will create a

four digit revision number. Alternatively, click Unlock and Discard Changes.

Note: An unreleased Standard cannot be tagged.

Click the Tag as Release (

) button.

Enter the Major, Minor and Revision numbers, respectively. Ensure that you

select an overall value higher than that of the current release or version.

3.

4.

5.

Click Release.

Note: As indicated in the screenshot, it is recommended that you validate a

Standard before tagging it for release. For information, see Validating

Standards.

CHAPTER 6 - Using Classes

6

Using Classes

A Class is an object that identifies the requirements of data attributes or elements with

their relationships and interpretations applied. By establishing Class hierarchies, you can

define and control the behaviour of the underlying instantiated objects.

6.1

Introducing Class Types

There are four Class types, Functional (

), Physical (

), Document (

) and General (

), that define the nature of the data they contain, respectively. Each Class type must be

categorised according to the details it holds. For information, see Class Concepts in Key

Concepts.

6.2

Creating Classes

To create a class or sub-class, ensure that you have the Creator privilege and have the

Standard locked for editing:

1.

2.

Right-click a Class Concept folder and select Add Class or Add Sub-Class.

Enter the required information:

a.

Enter a unique identification for the class. This supports alphanumeric

characters. This field does not auto-increment.

Note: Whilst the application accepts white spaces and special characters,

it is not recommended to use them.

b.

Enter a name for the identification in this optional field. If left blank, it

shows the ID as the name. This field accepts alphanumeric characters.

c.

Describe what the Class does for you.

3.

To create the record and continue to create more records, click Add.

Alternatively, click Add and Close. This brings you to the Details tab of that

Class.

4.

In the Corresponding Identifiers section on the Details tab, enter a

nomenclature map. You can enter more than one nomenclature map. For

information, see Implementing Corresponding Identifiers.

5.

Select the Abstract check box if the Class is generic and will include more

specific Classes.

Release 2.2.1

6. Define the Discipline for the Class. This is a text box unless the appropriate

Aspect has been associated to an Enumerated List. For information, see

Understanding Aspects. Otherwise, this displays as a drop-down menu.

7.

Enter a value in the Sort Order field. This value represents the order in which

Classes are listed under their parent folder in the tree view.

The Class is defined.

Notes:

· To discontinue using a Class, select the Obsolete check box in the General

Properties section on the Details tab.

· For details on adding and managing taxonomies in the Taxonomy Occurrences

tab, see Maintaining Taxonomies.

Changing the Parent Class

You can extend from only one Parent Class. To change an extension from a Parent Class:

1.

In the Class that you wish to move from one parent to another, go to the

Details tab.

2.

3.

Click the Plus button (

) and select the required Class from the dropdown.

In the Select Parent Class window, choose the required Parent Class from the

dropdown.

4.

Click Select.

CHAPTER 6 - Using Classes

6.4

Allocating Attributes to Classes

There are two ways in which you can apply an attribute to a Class. You can manually

allocate an attribute to a Class, which is described below, or a class can inherit all the

attributes allocated to its parent class. The inherited Classes contain a green inward arrow

sign on the icon. See Icon Annotations and the term Hierarchy in Key Concepts for

information.

To allocate an attribute to a Class, ensure that you have the Creator or Author privilege

and have the Standard locked for editing:

1.

In a Class that you want to allocate an Attribute, navigate to the Permissible

Attributes tab.

2.

3.

Click the Add button (

).

In the Add Permissible Attributes window, select the required attribute from

the dropdown.

4.

To create the record and continue to create records, click Add. Alternatively,

click Add and Close to add the record and close the window.

6.5

Exploring the Network

The Network tab is a visual representation of the relationships between objects. Use this

tab to drill down to the point where data is displayed in rounded rectangles, which are

data objects. You may click these objects to open a network of their own in a separate

tab.

For the top-level visual representation, click the Network View (

) button or use the

List View (

) button to view the the tabular format. The buttons appear as you hover

over a parent object. The List View (also called the Grid View) and Network View buttons

are displayed in the right-click menu on only the top-level item in the tree view for

Classes and Attributes.
